> Last updated: 2026-05-01

This file contains the full text content of all pages on newyorkbankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## 341 Meeting of Creditors in New York - What to Expect
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/341-meeting.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›341 Meeting

The 341 Meeting of Creditors in New York

The 341 meeting is required in every bankruptcy case, but it is usually brief and straightforward. Most last 5-10 minutes. Here is what to expect in the Southern District of New York.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

The 341 meeting of creditors (named after 11 U.S.C. section 341) is a required hearing in every bankruptcy case. The trustee assigned to your case - and any creditors who choose to attend - may ask you questions under oath about:

Your identity (verified by ID and Social Security documentation)

The accuracy of your petition and schedules

Your assets, income, and expenses

Recent financial transactions

Whether anything has changed since filing

The meeting is not held in a courtroom and the judge does not attend. It typically takes place in a conference room at the courthouse or by phone/video.

Common Questions the Trustee Asks

Is this your signature on the petition?

Did you read the petition before signing it?

Is everything in the petition true and correct?

Did you list all of your assets?

Did you list all of your debts?

Have you sold or transferred any property in the last two years?

Do you own any real estate?

Are you expecting a tax refund?

Are you entitled to any inheritance or insurance proceeds?

Do you have any pending lawsuits or claims?

Is there anything you want to add or correct?

Answer honestly and briefly. If you do not know the answer, say so. Your attorney is present to help.

What to Bring

Government-issued photo ID - Driver's license, passport, or state ID

Proof of Social Security number - Social Security card, recent tax return showing full SSN, or W-2

Copy of your petition - Your attorney will have this, but bring your own copy

Recent pay stubs - If requested by the trustee

Recent bank statements - If requested by the trustee

S.D.N.Y. Meeting Locations

341 meetings in the Southern District of New York are held at:

Manhattan: One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004 (or remote via phone/video)

White Plains: 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601 (or remote via phone/video)

Many S.D.N.Y. 341 meetings are currently conducted by telephone or video conference. Your attorney will confirm the format and provide dial-in or login information.

Tips for a Smooth Meeting

Review your petition beforehand. Know what is in it. The trustee's questions are based on what you filed.

Be on time. Arrive 15 minutes early for in-person meetings. Log in 5 minutes early for remote meetings.

Dress appropriately. Business casual is fine. You are not appearing before the judge.

Answer only what is asked. Short, truthful answers. Do not volunteer extra information.

Do not lie. You are under oath. Perjury in a bankruptcy case is a federal crime.

Stay calm. The meeting is routine. Most are finished in under 10 minutes.

What Happens After the Meeting

Chapter 7: If no issues arise, the trustee files a "no distribution" report (meaning no assets to liquidate). Discharge is typically entered 60-90 days after the meeting.

Chapter 13: The trustee reviews your proposed plan and may negotiate modifications. The confirmation hearing follows within 45-90 days.

Continued meeting: In some cases, the trustee may continue (reschedule) the meeting if additional documents are needed. This is not unusual and does not mean there is a problem.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A required hearing where the trustee asks questions under oath about your finances and petition. It happens 21-40 days after filing and usually lasts 5-10 minutes. Full guide.

What questions does the trustee ask?

Common questions: Is this your petition? Did you list all assets and debts? Have you transferred property recently? Are you expecting tax refunds or inheritances? Short, honest answers are sufficient.

Do creditors attend the 341 meeting?

Creditors have the right to attend but rarely do in consumer cases. If a creditor does attend, they may ask about specific debts. Your attorney is present to protect your interests.

What if I cannot attend?

Attendance is mandatory. If you have a legitimate conflict, your attorney can request a continuance. Missing the meeting without rescheduling can result in dismissal of your case.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Automatic Stay in New York Bankruptcy - Stop Collections, Garnishments, Foreclosure
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/automatic-stay.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Automatic Stay

The Automatic Stay in New York Bankruptcy

The moment you file bankruptcy, a powerful federal court order called the automatic stay stops nearly all creditor actions against you - collections, lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, and repossessions.

What the Automatic Stay Stops

Under 11 U.S.C. section 362, filing a bankruptcy petition immediately halts:

Wage garnishments - New York allows garnishment of 10% of gross wages or 25% of disposable income (whichever is less). The stay stops this immediately.

Foreclosure proceedings - New York uses judicial foreclosure, which typically takes 12-18 months. The stay halts this process at any stage.

Vehicle repossession - Creditors cannot repossess your car once the stay is in effect.

Lawsuits - All pending civil suits for debt collection are paused.

Collection calls and letters - Creditors must stop all contact.

Bank account levies - Frozen accounts are released.

Utility disconnections - Utilities cannot shut off service for at least 20 days after filing.

Eviction proceedings - May be paused in some circumstances (limited protection).

What the Automatic Stay Does NOT Stop

Certain actions continue despite the automatic stay:

Criminal proceedings - Pending criminal cases continue normally.

Child support and alimony collection - Domestic support obligations are not stayed.

Tax audits - The IRS can continue auditing (but cannot collect).

Pension loan repayments - Withholding from pension/retirement continues.

Eviction with judgment - If a landlord already has a judgment for possession, the stay may not help.

How It Works for New York Filers

Chapter 7

The stay lasts for the duration of your Chapter 7 case - typically 3-4 months. After discharge, the discharge injunction permanently prevents creditors from collecting discharged debts.

Chapter 13

The stay lasts throughout your entire 3-5 year repayment plan. This gives you time to catch up on mortgage payments through the plan while keeping your home.

Repeat Filers: Limited Stay Rules

If you had a prior bankruptcy case dismissed within the past year, special rules apply under 11 U.S.C. section 362(c):

One prior dismissal in the last year: The automatic stay expires after 30 days unless you file a motion to extend it and demonstrate good faith.

Two or more prior dismissals in the last year: No automatic stay at all - you must file a motion and obtain a court order imposing the stay.

The 1328(f) screener can help you check how prior filings affect your eligibility and protections.

Creditor Violations of the Stay

Any creditor who knowingly violates the automatic stay can face serious consequences:

Contempt of court - The bankruptcy court can hold violators in contempt.

Actual damages - You can recover the financial harm caused by the violation.

Emotional distress damages - In cases of willful violation.

Attorney fees - The violating creditor may be required to pay your legal costs.

Punitive damages - In egregious cases, courts may impose additional penalties.

Common violations in New York include continued wage garnishment after filing, repossession attempts, and collection calls. If a creditor violates the stay, notify your attorney (or the court if you are pro se) immediately.

New York Courthouses

The automatic stay is enforced by the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of New York:

Manhattan: One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

White Plains: 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

Cases are assigned based on the county where you live. Look up your district.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the automatic stay in bankruptcy?

The automatic stay is a federal court order under 11 U.S.C. section 362 that takes effect the instant you file your bankruptcy petition. It stops most creditor actions including lawsuits, wage garnishments, foreclosure sales, repossessions, and collection calls.

Does the automatic stay stop foreclosure in New York?

Yes. The stay immediately halts foreclosure proceedings, including scheduled sales. New York uses judicial foreclosure, which typically takes 12-18 months. Filing bankruptcy stops this process and gives you time to catch up on payments through a Chapter 13 plan.

How long does the automatic stay last?

In a standard first-time filing, the stay lasts throughout your entire bankruptcy case. In Chapter 7, that is typically 3-4 months. In Chapter 13, it lasts for the full 3-5 year plan. If you had a case dismissed within the past year, the stay may be limited to 30 days.

Can creditors violate the automatic stay?

Any creditor who continues collection activity after being notified of the filing violates the stay and may be held in contempt. Courts can award actual damages, attorney fees, and punitive damages for willful violations.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/bank-account.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in New York

Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in New York

Your bank account does not disappear when you file bankruptcy, but money in the account on the filing date is part of the estate. Here is how to protect it.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Happens to Your Bank Account

When you file bankruptcy, cash in your bank account on the petition date becomes property of the estate. The trustee will review your bank statements. However, you can protect funds with exemptions:

Federal wildcard: $1,475 per person, plus up to $13,950 of unused homestead exemption (if you do not own a home, this is very valuable)

New York cash exemption: Limited, which is why many non-homeowners choose federal exemptions

Social Security funds: Fully exempt regardless of which exemption scheme you choose

Veteran benefits, disability, unemployment: Generally fully exempt

Practical tip: Many attorneys advise filing when your bank balance is at its lowest point in the month - after paying rent and bills but before the next paycheck.

Bank Freezes and Setoff

If you have a credit card, overdraft, or loan with the same bank where you keep your checking account, the bank may attempt to set off (take) your account balance to pay what you owe them.

The automatic stay prevents setoff, but some banks freeze accounts first and ask questions later

A freeze can last days or weeks while the bank's legal department reviews the bankruptcy filing

During the freeze, checks bounce, autopay fails, and you cannot access your money

How to Avoid This

Open a new account at a bank where you have no debts before filing

Move your direct deposit to the new account

Transfer operating funds (but only normal amounts - do not drain accounts to hide money)

Keep the old account open with minimal balance until after the 341 meeting

New York Bank Levy Protections

Even before bankruptcy, New York law provides some protection. The first $3,600 in a bank account is automatically exempt from creditor levies under CPLR 5222-a (240 times the state minimum wage). This means creditors with judgments cannot touch the first $3,600.

Social Security, veterans benefits, disability, unemployment, and child support payments deposited in your account are also automatically exempt from levy under both state and federal law.

Filing bankruptcy adds the automatic stay, which stops all existing levies and prevents new ones.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will I lose my bank account if I file bankruptcy?

No. You keep the account. Money in it on the filing date is part of the estate but can be protected with exemptions. The federal wildcard or New York exemptions can protect reasonable balances.

Can the bank freeze my account?

If you owe that bank money, they may freeze your account temporarily. Avoid this by moving your primary account to a different bank before filing.

Is my direct deposit safe after filing?

Post-filing income is generally not part of the Chapter 7 estate. Your paychecks deposited after the filing date are yours. In Chapter 13, post-filing income funds your plan payments.

Can I open a new bank account after filing?

Yes. There is no restriction on opening bank accounts during or after bankruptcy. Some banks run ChexSystems checks, but most will allow you to open an account.

## Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/buy-car-chapter-13.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13?

Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13?

A free guide for New York residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

You need trustee approval and a court motion to purchase a vehicle during Chapter 13. The trustee will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary and whether the payment fits your budget. Interest rates are typically higher. Used vehicles are more likely to be approved.

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13?

You need trustee approval and a court motion to purchase a vehicle during Chapter 13. The trustee will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary and whether the payment fits your budget. Interest rates are typically higher. Used vehicles are more likely to be approved.

Does this apply in New York?

Yes. This applies to S.D.N.Y.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Buying a House After Bankruptcy in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/buying-a-house.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Buying a House After Bankruptcy in New York

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in New York

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent homeownership. With the right strategy, many New Yorkers qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years of discharge.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Mortgage Waiting Periods

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year of on-time payments (with court approval)

VA2 years from discharge1 year of on-time payments (with court approval)

USDA3 years from discharge1 year of on-time payments (with court approval)

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

Waiting periods may be reduced with documented extenuating circumstances (medical emergency, job loss due to employer bankruptcy, etc.).

Rebuilding Credit for a Mortgage

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge. Use it for small purchases and pay in full monthly.

Become an authorized user on a family member's account with good history.

Get a credit-builder loan from a credit union - these report to all three bureaus.

Pay every bill on time. Payment history is 35% of your FICO score.

Keep utilization below 30%. Lower is better - aim for under 10%.

Do not apply for too much credit. Each hard inquiry temporarily reduces your score.

Many people reach a 620-680 credit score within 2-3 years of discharge, sufficient for FHA loans.

New York Housing Considerations

Homestead exemption. When you buy your new home, New York's homestead exemption ($179,975--$215,550 by county) will protect your equity in a future financial crisis.

High property taxes. Factor New York's property taxes into your affordability calculation. Lenders include taxes in your debt-to-income ratio.

Co-ops vs condos. Many New York City buildings are co-ops, which have their own financial approval process separate from the mortgage. Co-op boards can be stricter about bankruptcy history than lenders.

Down payment assistance. New York has programs like SONYMA (State of New York Mortgage Agency) that offer down payment assistance for first-time buyers, which may be available after the waiting period.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long after bankruptcy can I buy a house?

FHA: 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into Chapter 13 with court approval. VA: same. Conventional: 4 years after Chapter 7 or 2 years after Chapter 13 discharge. USDA: 3 years after Chapter 7.

Can I get an FHA loan after bankruptcy?

Yes. FHA loans are the most accessible after bankruptcy. Wait 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge. During Chapter 13, apply after 1 year of on-time payments with court approval. Minimum credit score: 580 for 3.5% down, 500 for 10% down.

How does bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Chapter 7 stays on your report for 10 years, Chapter 13 for 7 years, but impact diminishes over time. Many people reach 620-680 within 2-3 years by building credit responsibly. Read more at Credit After Bankruptcy.

Can I buy a house during Chapter 13?

Yes, but you need court approval. Most trustees and judges require at least 1 year of on-time plan payments, evidence that you can afford the new mortgage, and that the purchase will not harm your ability to complete the plan.

Check Your Discharge Status

## Car Leases and Bankruptcy
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/car-lease.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Car Leases and Bankruptcy

Car Leases and Bankruptcy

A free guide for New York residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

You have 60 days after filing to decide whether to assume (keep) or reject (surrender) your car lease. If you are current on payments, you can typically keep the lease by assuming it. If behind, rejection discharges the remaining obligation. Lease buyout may be an option if you want to own the vehicle.

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Car Leases and Bankruptcy

You have 60 days after filing to decide whether to assume (keep) or reject (surrender) your car lease. If you are current on payments, you can typically keep the lease by assuming it. If behind, rejection discharges the remaining obligation. Lease buyout may be an option if you want to own the vehicle.

Does this apply in New York?

Yes. This applies to S.D.N.Y.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Chapter 13 Eligibility in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/chapter-13-eligibility.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Chapter 13 Eligibility in New York

Chapter 13 Eligibility in New York

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Unlike Chapter 7, there is no means test income limit - but there are other requirements.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Core Requirements

Regular income. You must have income sufficiently stable and regular to make monthly plan payments. W-2 employment, self-employment, Social Security, disability, pension, rental income, and regular family support all count.

Debt limits. Your total debts (secured + unsecured combined) must be under $2,750,000. If your debts exceed this limit, Chapter 11 may be an option.

Credit counseling. You must complete an approved credit counseling course within 180 days before filing.

Tax filing. You must be current on tax return filings for the last 4 years.

Not a business entity. Only individuals (and married couples filing jointly) can file Chapter 13. Corporations and LLCs must use Chapter 7 or 11.

Plan Length: 3 Years vs 5 Years

Below-Median Income

If your household income is below New York's median ($66,973 for 1 person), your plan can be as short as 3 years (36 months). You may propose a 5-year plan if needed to fit all required payments.

Above-Median Income

If your income exceeds the median, your plan must be 5 years (60 months). All projected disposable income must go toward plan payments.

Prior Filing Restrictions

Prior Chapter 13 discharge: Wait 2 years before receiving another Chapter 13 discharge - 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f)(2)

Prior Chapter 7 discharge: Wait 4 years before receiving a Chapter 13 discharge - section 1328(f)(1)

Prior dismissal within 1 year: The automatic stay is limited to 30 days unless extended by court order - section 362(c)(3)

Two or more dismissals in 1 year: No automatic stay at all without a court order - section 362(c)(4)

Use the 1328(f) screener to check whether prior filings affect your eligibility.

What Chapter 13 Can Do That Chapter 7 Cannot

Save your home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments over the plan

Cram down vehicle loans - reduce the loan balance to the vehicle's current value if the loan is more than 910 days old

Discharge some debts that survive Chapter 7, including certain property settlement debts from divorce

Protect co-signers through the co-debtor stay (section 1301)

Strip off junior liens on your home if the home's value does not support them

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the debt limits for Chapter 13?

The combined debt limit is $2,750,000 (secured and unsecured combined). If your debts exceed this, consider Chapter 11.

Do I need a job to file Chapter 13?

You need "regular income," not necessarily a job. Social Security, disability, pension, rental income, self-employment, and regular family contributions all qualify.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan?

3 years if your income is below New York's median ($66,973 for 1 person). 5 years if above the median. The plan cannot exceed 5 years.

Can I file Chapter 13 after a prior discharge?

You can file, but receiving a discharge requires waiting 2 years after a prior Chapter 13 discharge or 4 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Filing without discharge eligibility is sometimes used to restructure secured debts (a "Chapter 20" strategy).

Check Your Eligibility

## Chapter 13 Payments in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/chapter-13-payments.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Chapter 13 Payments in New York

Chapter 13 Payments in New York

Your Chapter 13 plan payment is based on your income, debts, and what creditors would receive in Chapter 7. Here is how it works.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Your Payment Is Calculated

Your Chapter 13 payment must satisfy three tests:

Disposable income test. All projected disposable income (income minus allowed expenses) must go to the plan. Above-median filers ($66,973+ for 1 person in NY) use the means test expense deductions. Below-median filers use actual expenses.

Best-interests test. Unsecured creditors must receive at least as much as they would get in a Chapter 7 liquidation. If you have non-exempt assets worth $10,000, unsecured creditors must get at least $10,000 over the plan.

Feasibility test. You must actually be able to make the proposed payment given your budget.

Where Your Payment Goes

The Chapter 13 trustee receives your payment and distributes it:

Trustee commission: Typically 5-10% of plan payments

Attorney fees: Your attorney's fees are often paid through the plan

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage catch-up payments, car loan payments

Priority debts: Recent tax debts and child support arrears (must be paid in full)

Unsecured creditors: Credit cards, medical bills, personal loans (receive whatever is left, often 0-10%)

Your regular ongoing mortgage payment is usually paid directly by you, not through the plan (though some districts require plan payment of the ongoing mortgage).

Missing Payments and Modifications

If You Miss a Payment

The trustee may file a motion to dismiss your case

You typically have a short window to catch up before the hearing

Repeated delinquency signals the plan is not feasible

If dismissed, you lose automatic stay protection and creditors resume collection

Modifying Your Plan

Income drop: File a motion to reduce payments or extend the plan (up to 60 months max)

Income increase: The trustee or creditors may request higher payments

New emergency expense: Medical bills, car repairs - can justify modification

Conversion option: You can convert to Chapter 7 if you qualify and can no longer afford any plan

Frequently Asked Questions

How much will my Chapter 13 payment be?

It depends on your disposable income, secured debts, priority debts, and what unsecured creditors would receive in Chapter 7. Payments typically range from $200 to $2,000+ per month.

What happens if I miss a payment?

The trustee may move to dismiss your case. Contact your attorney immediately if you anticipate missing a payment - a plan modification is usually better than dismissal.

Can I pay off my plan early?

Yes, but paying off early means unsecured creditors may receive less than projected. Below-median filers can pay off early more easily. Above-median filers must commit all disposable income for 5 years, so early payoff may trigger a review.

Are payments made by payroll deduction?

Many S.D.N.Y. Chapter 13 plans use wage orders (payroll deductions) to ensure consistent payments. This is often preferred by trustees because it reduces the risk of missed payments.

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in New York - Payment Plans, Dismissal Rates, Timeline
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in New York

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. But in the Southern District of New York, the dismissal rate is significant - meaning many filers never complete their plan.

59.0% of S.D.N.Y. Chapter 13 cases are dismissed

59.0%

S.D.N.Y. Dismissal Rate

41.0%

Discharge Rate

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors according to the plan.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

S.D.N.Y. Dismissal Data

A dismissed Chapter 13 means the debtor spent months or years making payments but received no discharge - no debt relief.

Out of 25,112 Chapter 13 cases filed in the Southern District of New York between 2008 and 2024:

59.0% were dismissed before plan completion

41.0% achieved a successful discharge

24.9% of filers had a prior bankruptcy case

18.9% of filers represented themselves (pro se)

Why it matters: A dismissed Chapter 13 means the automatic stay ends, creditors resume collection, and the debtor receives no discharge. Strong legal representation is critical to completing a Chapter 13 plan. View full statistics.

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills, etc.

Plan Duration: 3 or 5 Years?

If your income is below the state median, you may be able to complete a 3-year plan. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The court will not approve a plan longer than 5 years.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. This eliminates remaining eligible unsecured debts.

If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent you from receiving another discharge.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 - Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate in New York?

The Southern District of New York has a 59.0% Chapter 13 dismissal rate. These rates matter because a dismissed case means no debt relief despite months or years of payments.

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Plans last 3 to 5 years. If your income is below the state median, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above, your plan must be 5 years.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. You can keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan. The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

The automatic stay ends, creditors resume collection, and you receive no discharge. You may be able to file again, but there may be waiting periods.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Chapter 7 Eligibility in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/chapter-7-eligibility.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Chapter 7 Eligibility in New York

Chapter 7 Eligibility in New York

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months, but you must qualify. The primary gatekeeper is the means test. Here is what New York filers need to know.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

New York Means Test Income Limits

The means test compares your average monthly income over the last 6 calendar months to New York's median income. If you are below the median, you automatically qualify.

Household SizeAnnual Median IncomeMonthly Equivalent

1 person$66,973$5,581

2 persons$82,282$6,857

3 persons$97,878$8,157

4 persons$119,164$9,930

Add $10,300 for each person beyond 4. Figures updated periodically by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Above-Median Income? You May Still Qualify

If your income exceeds the median, the means test subtracts allowed expenses (housing, transportation, healthcare, taxes, childcare, etc.) from your income. If the remaining disposable income is too low to fund a meaningful Chapter 13 plan, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

Key deductions include:

Actual mortgage or rent payments

IRS Local Standards for transportation and housing

Health insurance and out-of-pocket medical costs

Childcare and education expenses (up to $2,025/child/year)

Payroll taxes and mandatory retirement contributions

Secured debt payments (car loans, mortgage)

Priority debts (tax debts, domestic support obligations)

New York's high cost of living often means above-median filers still pass after deductions.

Who Cannot File Chapter 7

Fail the means test with no special circumstances - you would need to file Chapter 13 instead

Prior Chapter 7 discharge within the last 8 years (measured from filing date to filing date) - 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8)

Prior Chapter 13 discharge within the last 6 years (unless you paid 100% of unsecured claims or 70% in a good-faith plan) - section 727(a)(9)

Prior dismissal for abuse within 180 days - section 109(g)

No credit counseling - you must complete an approved credit counseling course within 180 days before filing

Stockbrokers and commodity brokers - must use SIPA liquidation instead

New York Exemption Choice

New York lets you choose between state and federal exemptions. Key state exemptions:

Homestead: $179,975--$215,550 depending on county

Vehicle: $4,825

Personal property: Various limits for clothing, furniture, tools of trade

The federal exemption set has a homestead of $27,900 (individual) but offers a more generous wildcard exemption ($1,475 + up to $13,950 of unused homestead). If you do not own a home, federal exemptions may protect more of your other property.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in New York?

The means test uses New York's median income: $66,973 for 1 person, $82,282 for 2, $97,878 for 3, $119,164 for 4. Below the median means automatic qualification. Above-median filers may still qualify after allowed expense deductions.

Can I choose between state and federal exemptions?

Yes. New York allows debtors to choose. State homestead: $179,975--$215,550 by county. Federal homestead: $27,900. The federal wildcard may be better if you do not own a home.

How often can I file Chapter 7?

You must wait 8 years between Chapter 7 filing dates. If you received a Chapter 13 discharge, you must wait 6 years unless you paid 100% of unsecured claims or 70% in a good-faith plan. Check with the 1328(f) screener.

Where do I file in New York?

The S.D.N.Y. has courthouses at One Bowling Green in Manhattan and 300 Quarropas Street in White Plains. Filing is typically done electronically through an attorney's CM/ECF account.

Check Your Eligibility

Find out whether prior filings affect your discharge eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in New York - Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in New York

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what New York residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors - but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In New York, the vast majority of Chapter 7 filers keep all their property because it falls within New York exemption limits.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the state median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility

The median income figures are updated periodically. For a single filer in New York, the current median is approximately $67,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

New York Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep. New York gives you the choice between state and federal exemptions. Many filers choose state exemptions because of the higher homestead protection, but federal exemptions offer a more generous wildcard.

PropertyNew York Exemption

Homestead$179,975 - $215,550 (varies by county) equity

Vehicle$4,825

Household goodsNo per-item limit

Tools of trade$3,000

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Public benefitsFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation - Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing - Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately - creditors must stop all collection activity.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting - Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education - Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge - The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, or debts from fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7?

The means test compares your household income to the New York median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed calculation determines eligibility.

How long does Chapter 7 take in New York?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in New York?

New York exemptions protect homestead equity up to $179,975 - $215,550 (varies by county), one vehicle up to $4,825, household goods, tools of trade, and retirement accounts. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for New York - Documents and Steps
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/checklist.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Checklist

Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for New York

Everything you need to gather and complete before filing bankruptcy in the Southern District of New York. Print this page and check off items as you go.

Required Documents

Income Documentation

Pay stubs - last 6 months (all jobs)

Federal tax returns - last 2 years (with all schedules)

New York State tax returns - last 2 years

NYC tax returns - last 2 years (if applicable)

Social Security or pension statements - if receiving benefits

Self-employment records - profit/loss statements, 1099s

Other income documentation - alimony, rental income, side jobs

Bank and Financial Accounts

Bank statements - last 6 months (all accounts, including closed ones)

Investment account statements - stocks, bonds, mutual funds

Retirement account statements - 401(k), IRA, pension (most recent)

Life insurance policies - with cash value information

Debt Documentation

Credit report - pull free copies from all 3 bureaus (AnnualCreditReport.com)

List of all creditors - with account numbers, balances, and addresses

Collection letters and notices

Lawsuit papers - any pending or recent lawsuits

Judgment records - any money judgments against you

Garnishment orders - current or recent

Property and Asset Documentation

Mortgage statements - current balance, monthly payment

Property deed or co-op proprietary lease

Property tax assessment - current market value

Vehicle titles and registration

Vehicle loan/lease agreements

Estimated values of vehicles (use KBB or NADA)

Valuable personal property - jewelry, electronics, collections

Other Documents

Government-issued photo ID - for the 341 meeting

Social Security card or proof of SSN

Domestic support orders - child support, alimony

Divorce decree or separation agreement (if applicable)

Insurance policies - homeowner's, auto, health

Lease or rental agreement

Required Steps Before Filing

Complete credit counseling course - Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Costs $15-25. Can be done online in about 1 hour. Must be completed within 180 days before filing. Keep the certificate.

Choose state or federal exemptions - New York allows you to pick one system. State exemptions are better for homeowners (homestead $179,975-$215,550). Federal may be better for renters (larger wildcard).

Determine which chapter to file - Chapter 7 (debt elimination, 3-4 months) or Chapter 13 (repayment plan, 3-5 years). The means test determines Chapter 7 eligibility.

Pull your credit reports - Verify all debts are accounted for. Dispute errors. Free at AnnualCreditReport.com.

Stop certain activities - No new credit card charges, no property transfers, no repaying family loans, no cash advances.

After Filing: Remaining Steps

Attend the 341 meeting - Usually 21-40 days after filing. Bring photo ID and SSN proof.

Complete debtor education course - Second required course ($10-25). Must be completed before discharge. File the certificate with the court.

Provide tax return to trustee - The trustee may request your most recent tax return (must be provided within 7 days of request).

Respond to trustee requests - Provide any additional documents the trustee requests promptly.

Make plan payments (Chapter 13 only) - First payment due within 30 days of filing.

Things to Avoid Before Filing

Do not run up credit card debt. Purchases over $800 for luxury goods within 90 days may be challenged as non-dischargeable.

Do not take cash advances. Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are presumptively non-dischargeable.

Do not transfer property. Moving assets to friends, family, or trusts can be reversed by the trustee and may constitute fraud.

Do not repay family loans. Paying back a relative within a year before filing is a preferential transfer. The trustee can recover the money from your relative.

Do not empty retirement accounts. Retirement funds are fully exempt in bankruptcy - using them to pay debts first is unnecessary.

Do not hide assets. Concealing assets is a federal crime that can result in denial of discharge and criminal prosecution.

Free Legal Help in New York

If you cannot afford an attorney, these organizations provide free bankruptcy assistance:

Legal Aid Society - Free legal services for low-income NYC residents

NYLAG (New York Legal Assistance Group) - Free bankruptcy clinics and representation

Legal Services NYC - Consumer debt and bankruptcy help in all five boroughs

Full list of New York legal aid resources.

Frequently Asked Questions

What documents do I need to file bankruptcy?

Tax returns (2 years), pay stubs (6 months), bank statements (6 months), list of all debts and assets, vehicle titles, mortgage statements, and your credit counseling certificate. Full guide.

Do I need to take a credit counseling course?

Yes, two courses are required: credit counseling before filing ($15-25, about 1 hour online) and debtor education after filing but before discharge ($10-25). Both must be from DOJ-approved providers.

How long does it take to prepare?

Usually 1-4 weeks. Emergency filings can be done in a few days if facing imminent garnishment, repossession, or foreclosure. See full timeline.

What should I stop doing before filing?

Avoid running up new debt, transferring property, repaying family loans, taking cash advances, or selling assets below market value. These can create problems in your case.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

What to Expect

## Bankruptcy in Bronx, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/bronx.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Bronx, NY

Bankruptcy in Bronx, NY

The Bronx is the northernmost borough of New York City and the only borough on the U.S. mainland. Bronx County is home to approximately 1.4 million residents, Yankee Stadium, the Bronx Zoo, and the New York Botanical Garden.

Key Information

County

Bronx County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Southern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from Bronx are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

10451
10452
10453
10454
10455
10456
10457
10458
10459
10460
10461
10462
10463
10464
10465
10466
10467
10468
10469
10470
10471
10472
10473
10474
10475

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in Bronx

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Bronx County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Bronx, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Southern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Bronx

Bronx Legal Services - Free legal help for Bronx residents

Legal Aid Society - Bronx - Free legal assistance for qualifying residents

Bronx Bar Association Volunteer Lawyers Project - Pro bono attorneys for qualifying individuals

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Bronx residents file bankruptcy?

Bronx residents file in the Southern District of New York at One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004 (Lower Manhattan). This is different from Brooklyn and Queens, which are in the Eastern District. The courthouse is accessible via the 4/5 trains to Bowling Green station.

What exemptions do Bronx residents get?

New York allows a choice of state or federal exemptions. The state homestead exemption for Bronx County is $179,975. Federal exemptions offer $27,900 homestead but a more generous wildcard ($1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead). Renters often benefit more from federal exemptions.

Can bankruptcy stop an eviction in the Bronx?

The automatic stay provides limited protection against eviction. If your landlord already has a judgment for possession before you file, the stay may not prevent eviction. If the eviction is based solely on unpaid rent, the stay is temporary - you must pay rent that comes due after filing to maintain the stay.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Brooklyn, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/brooklyn.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Brooklyn, NY

Bankruptcy in Brooklyn, NY

Brooklyn is the most populous borough of New York City, with approximately 2.7 million residents in Kings County. It is home to diverse communities ranging from Williamsburg to Bay Ridge, each with distinct character.

Key Information

County

Kings County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from Brooklyn are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

11201
11203
11204
11205
11206
11207
11208
11209
11210
11211
11212
11213
11214
11215
11216
11217
11218
11219
11220
11221
11222
11223
11224
11225
11226
11228
11229
11230
11231
11232
11233
11234
11235
11236
11237
11238
11239

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in Brooklyn

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Kings County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Brooklyn, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Eastern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Brooklyn

Brooklyn Legal Services - Free legal help for Brooklyn residents

Legal Aid Society - Brooklyn - Free legal assistance for qualifying residents

Brooklyn Bar Association Volunteer Lawyers Project - Pro bono attorneys for qualifying individuals

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Brooklyn residents file bankruptcy?

Brooklyn residents file in the Eastern District of New York at 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201. This is conveniently located in downtown Brooklyn, accessible by many subway lines. All 341 meetings are also held at this location.

What exemptions apply in Brooklyn?

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal exemptions. The state homestead exemption for Kings County is $179,975 - generally better than the federal $27,900. However, federal exemptions may be better for renters due to the more generous wildcard ($1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead).

Can I keep my Brooklyn apartment in bankruptcy?

Bankruptcy does not affect your rental lease. You have the right to assume (keep) your lease in Chapter 7. If you are behind on rent, the landlord may seek relief from the automatic stay. For cooperative apartments (co-ops), the shares are treated as real property under the homestead exemption.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Freeport, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/freeport.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Freeport, NY

Bankruptcy in Freeport, NY

Freeport is a large village in Nassau County on the South Shore of Long Island. Known as the Nautical Mile for its waterfront dining and boating district, it is a diverse community of approximately 45,000 residents.

Key Information

County

Nassau County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from Freeport are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

11520

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in Freeport

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Nassau County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Freeport, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Eastern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Freeport

Nassau/Suffolk Law Services - Free legal help for Long Island residents

Legal Aid Society of Nassau County - Free legal assistance for qualifying residents

Freeport Community Resources - Local government assistance and referrals

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Freeport residents file bankruptcy?

Freeport residents file in the Eastern District of New York at 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201. The LIRR Babylon line runs from Freeport to Atlantic Terminal in Brooklyn, making the courthouse accessible by public transit.

Can I keep my boat in bankruptcy if I live in Freeport?

Boats are personal property subject to exemptions. Under federal exemptions, a boat is not specifically exempted but may be protected by the wildcard ($1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead). Under New York state exemptions, there is no specific boat exemption. High-value boats may be at risk in Chapter 7.

How does bankruptcy affect my Freeport property taxes?

Property taxes are not dischargeable. Nassau County property taxes (among the highest in the nation) must continue to be paid. In Chapter 13, past-due property taxes can be included in your repayment plan. Tax lien sales are halted by the automatic stay during the case.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Hempstead, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/hempstead.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Hempstead, NY

Bankruptcy in Hempstead, NY

Hempstead is one of the most populous villages in New York State, located in Nassau County on Long Island. The Town of Hempstead (which includes the village and surrounding areas) is one of the most populous townships in the United States.

Key Information

County

Nassau County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from Hempstead are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

11549
11550
11551

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in Hempstead

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Nassau County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Hempstead, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Eastern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Hempstead

Nassau/Suffolk Law Services - Free legal help for Long Island residents

Legal Aid Society of Nassau County - Free legal assistance for qualifying residents

Hempstead Community Development Agency - Financial counseling and assistance programs

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Hempstead residents file bankruptcy?

Hempstead residents file in the Eastern District of New York at 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201. The drive from Hempstead is approximately 35-45 minutes west, or accessible via LIRR to Atlantic Terminal and a short walk.

What homestead exemption applies in Hempstead?

New York's state homestead exemption for Nassau County is $179,975. Long Island home values are often high, so many homeowners have equity exceeding this amount. Chapter 13 may be the better option to protect your home if your equity exceeds the exemption.

Is Hempstead in the same district as Brooklyn?

Yes. Both Hempstead (Nassau County) and Brooklyn (Kings County) are in the Eastern District of New York. Cases from all of Long Island, Brooklyn, Queens, and Staten Island are filed at the Brooklyn courthouse.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy by City - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities

Bankruptcy by City - New York City Area

Find bankruptcy information specific to your borough or suburb in the New York City metro area. Manhattan, Bronx, and Westchester file in the Southern District (S.D.N.Y.). Brooklyn, Queens, Staten Island, and Long Island file in the Eastern District (E.D.N.Y.). Each page includes the correct courthouse, exemptions, and local resources.

Select Your City

Click on your city below for courthouse location, exemptions, zip codes, local resources, and frequently asked questions specific to your community.

Kings County

Queens, NY

Bronx, NY

Richmond County

Westchester County

Westchester County

## Bankruptcy in Mount Vernon, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/mount-vernon.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Mount Vernon, NY

Bankruptcy in Mount Vernon, NY

Mount Vernon is a city in Westchester County directly north of the Bronx border. It is one of the most densely populated cities in New York State outside of New York City, with approximately 75,000 residents.

Key Information

County

Westchester County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Southern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from Mount Vernon are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

10550
10551
10552
10553

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in Mount Vernon

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Westchester County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Mount Vernon, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Southern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Mount Vernon

Legal Services of the Hudson Valley - Free legal help for qualifying residents

Mount Vernon Community Resources - Local government assistance and referrals

Westchester County Department of Social Services - Financial assistance programs

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Mount Vernon residents file bankruptcy?

Mount Vernon residents file in the Southern District of New York, White Plains Division, at 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601. The courthouse is approximately 20-25 minutes north.

How does Mount Vernon's proximity to the Bronx affect filing?

Despite bordering the Bronx, Mount Vernon is in Westchester County and files in the White Plains Division, not the Manhattan courthouse. Different local rules and trustees may apply. You must file in the correct division based on your actual residence.

Can bankruptcy help with Mount Vernon tax liens?

Federal, state, and local tax liens survive Chapter 7 bankruptcy - the lien remains on your property even after the underlying debt is discharged. In Chapter 13, you can pay past-due taxes through your repayment plan over 3-5 years while stopping penalties and interest from accumulating.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in New Rochelle, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/new-rochelle.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » New Rochelle, NY

Bankruptcy in New Rochelle, NY

New Rochelle is a city in Westchester County on the Long Island Sound, approximately 16 miles north of Midtown Manhattan. It has a diverse population of approximately 80,000 and a mix of urban and suburban neighborhoods.

Key Information

County

Westchester County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Southern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from New Rochelle are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

10801
10802
10803
10804
10805

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in New Rochelle

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Westchester County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in New Rochelle, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Southern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in New Rochelle

Legal Services of the Hudson Valley - Free legal help for qualifying residents

Westchester County Legal Aid Society - Free legal assistance for qualifying residents

New Rochelle Community Resources - Local government assistance and referrals

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do New Rochelle residents file bankruptcy?

New Rochelle residents file in the Southern District of New York, White Plains Division, at 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601. The courthouse is approximately 15-20 minutes north of New Rochelle.

What is the homestead exemption in New Rochelle?

New York's state homestead exemption for Westchester County is $179,975 per debtor. Married couples filing jointly may protect up to $359,950 in home equity. For New Rochelle homes with higher equity, Chapter 13 is the safer option to protect your property.

Can I keep my retirement accounts in New Rochelle?

Yes. Retirement accounts are fully exempt in both New York state and federal exemptions. This includes 401(k)s, IRAs, pensions, 403(b)s, and other qualified plans. There is no cap on the amount protected. This is one of the strongest protections available to New Rochelle residents.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Queens, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/queens.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Queens, NY

Bankruptcy in Queens, NY

Queens is the most ethnically diverse urban area in the world and the largest borough of New York City by area. Queens County is home to over 2.3 million residents, JFK and LaGuardia airports, and Citi Field.

Key Information

County

Queens County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from Queens are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

11004
11101
11102
11103
11104
11105
11106
11354
11355
11356
11357
11358
11359
11360
11361
11362
11363
11364
11365
11366
11367
11368
11369
11370
11371
11372
11373
11374
11375
11377
11378
11379
11385
11411
11412
11413
11414
11415
11416
11417
11418
11419
11420
11421
11422
11423
11426
11427
11428
11429
11430
11432
11433
11434
11435
11436
11691
11692
11693
11694
11697

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in Queens

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Queens County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Queens, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Eastern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Queens

Queens Legal Services - Free legal help for Queens residents

Legal Aid Society - Queens - Free legal assistance for qualifying residents

Queens Volunteer Lawyers Project - Pro bono attorneys for qualifying individuals

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Queens residents file bankruptcy?

Queens residents file in the Eastern District of New York. The courthouse is at 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201. While not in Queens itself, it is accessible via subway from most Queens neighborhoods.

What homestead exemption applies in Queens?

New York's state homestead exemption for Queens County is $179,975. You can alternatively choose federal exemptions ($27,900 homestead but larger wildcard). Most Queens homeowners benefit from the state exemption. For co-ops, the shares are treated as real property.

Can I file bankruptcy in multiple languages in Queens?

Court documents must be filed in English, but the court provides interpreter services for 341 meetings and hearings in virtually any language. Queens' diverse legal community includes attorneys who practice in Chinese, Spanish, Korean, Hindi, Urdu, and many other languages.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Staten Island, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/staten-island.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Staten Island, NY

Bankruptcy in Staten Island, NY

Staten Island is the southernmost and least densely populated borough of New York City. Richmond County has a more suburban character than the other boroughs, with higher homeownership rates and a population of approximately 475,000.

Key Information

County

Richmond County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Eastern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from Staten Island are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

10301
10302
10303
10304
10305
10306
10307
10308
10309
10310
10311
10312
10314

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in Staten Island

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Richmond County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Staten Island, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Eastern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Staten Island

Legal Aid Society - Staten Island - Free legal assistance for qualifying residents

Staten Island Legal Services - Free legal help for Staten Island residents

Richmond County Bar Association - Attorney referral services

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Staten Island residents file bankruptcy?

Staten Island residents file in the Eastern District of New York at 271 Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201. While less convenient than a local courthouse, it is accessible via the Staten Island Ferry and subway.

Can I protect my Staten Island home in bankruptcy?

New York's state homestead exemption for Richmond County is $179,975. Staten Island has higher homeownership rates and home values than other boroughs. If your equity exceeds the exemption, Chapter 13 allows you to keep your home while repaying debts over 3-5 years.

Is Staten Island in a different district than Brooklyn?

No. Staten Island (Richmond County) and Brooklyn (Kings County) are both in the Eastern District of New York. The Bronx and Manhattan are in the Southern District. This matters because different districts may have different local rules and trustee practices.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in White Plains, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/white-plains.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » White Plains, NY

Bankruptcy in White Plains, NY

White Plains is the county seat of Westchester County, located approximately 25 miles north of Midtown Manhattan. It is a major commercial center with a growing downtown residential population.

Key Information

County

Westchester County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Southern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from White Plains are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

10601
10602
10603
10604
10605
10606
10607

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in White Plains

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Westchester County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in White Plains, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Southern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in White Plains

Legal Services of the Hudson Valley - Free legal help for qualifying residents

Westchester County Legal Aid Society - Free legal assistance at the courthouse

White Plains Community Resources - Local government referral services

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do White Plains residents file bankruptcy?

White Plains residents file in the Southern District of New York at 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601 - the bankruptcy courthouse is right in downtown White Plains. This is one of the most convenient locations in the metro area for local residents.

What is the advantage of the White Plains courthouse?

White Plains has its own bankruptcy courthouse at 300 Quarropas St, separate from the Manhattan courthouse. This means White Plains residents can attend 341 meetings and hearings locally without traveling to Manhattan. The court handles cases from all of Westchester County.

Can White Plains residents choose between state and federal exemptions?

Yes. New York is one of the states that allows this choice. You must pick one system - you cannot mix and match. Generally, homeowners benefit from state exemptions ($179,975 homestead) while renters may benefit from federal exemptions (larger wildcard for personal property).

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener
