## Bankruptcy in Yonkers, NY - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cities/yonkers.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Yonkers, NY

Bankruptcy in Yonkers, NY

Yonkers is the fourth-largest city in New York State, located in Westchester County directly north of the Bronx. It is the most populous city in Westchester County with approximately 200,000 residents.

Key Information

County

Westchester County

Your county determines your federal district and local courthouse.

Federal District

Southern District of New York

All bankruptcy cases from Yonkers are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

10701
10702
10703
10704
10705
10710

Verify your zip code falls within the correct filing district.

New York Exemptions (State or Federal) in Yonkers

New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 in Westchester County. Federal homestead: $27,900. State exemptions include: vehicle $4,550, cash $5,000, household goods up to $14,375, tools of trade $11,975, and jewelry $1,150. Federal exemptions offer: $27,900 homestead, $4,450 vehicle, and a wildcard of $1,475 plus up to $13,950 unused homestead. Retirement accounts are fully exempt under both systems.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Yonkers, NY

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - Filed with the Southern District of New York. The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at or near 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Local Resources in Yonkers

Legal Services of the Hudson Valley - Free legal help for qualifying Westchester residents

Westchester County Legal Aid Society - Free legal assistance for qualifying residents

Yonkers Community Action Program - Financial counseling and emergency assistance

These organizations may offer free or low-cost assistance. Contact them directly to verify current services and eligibility requirements.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Yonkers residents file bankruptcy?

Yonkers residents file in the Southern District of New York, White Plains Division, at 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601. The courthouse is approximately 20 minutes north of Yonkers. Some matters may be heard at One Bowling Green in Lower Manhattan.

What homestead exemption applies in Yonkers?

New York's state homestead exemption for Westchester County is $179,975. Given Yonkers' real estate values, many homeowners have equity exceeding this amount. Chapter 13 allows you to keep your home while repaying debts over 3-5 years regardless of equity.

Is Yonkers in the same district as the Bronx?

Yes. Both Yonkers (Westchester County) and the Bronx are in the Southern District of New York. However, Yonkers cases are typically handled in the White Plains Division while Bronx cases go to the Manhattan courthouse.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Converting Between Bankruptcy Chapters in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/converting-chapters.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Converting Between Bankruptcy Chapters in New York

Converting Between Bankruptcy Chapters in New York

Circumstances change. If you filed under the wrong chapter or your situation has shifted, you may be able to convert your case. Here is how conversion works in the Southern District of New York.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Conversion Options

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7

Absolute right under 11 U.S.C. section 1307(a) if case was not previously converted from Chapter 7

Conversion fee: $25

Must still pass the means test

Common when plan payments become unaffordable

Undistributed trustee funds are returned to you

Chapter 7 to Chapter 13

One-time right under 11 U.S.C. section 706(a) if case was not previously converted

Conversion fee: $25

Must have regular income and debts within Chapter 13 limits

Common when you need to save a home or vehicle

Must file a repayment plan within 15 days

Common Reasons to Convert

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7

Income dropped (job loss, reduction in hours, medical leave)

Plan payments are no longer affordable

You no longer need to protect a home or vehicle through the plan

The case is headed for dismissal and Chapter 7 is a better outcome

Chapter 7 to Chapter 13

You are about to lose a home or vehicle to liquidation

You need the automatic stay to last longer than 3-4 months

You have non-exempt assets the trustee would sell in Chapter 7

You want to catch up on mortgage or car payments through a plan

The Conversion Process in S.D.N.Y.

File a motion to convert with the bankruptcy court (or a notice of conversion if it is your absolute right)

Pay the $25 conversion fee

File amended schedules if your financial situation has changed since the original filing

New 341 meeting will be scheduled - the trustee may change

If converting to Chapter 13, file a repayment plan within 15 days

If converting to Chapter 7, the new means test is applied based on your current income

Important Limitations

One conversion per case. If you converted from Chapter 7 to Chapter 13, you cannot convert back to Chapter 7 as a matter of right - the court would need to approve it for cause.

Means test still applies. Converting to Chapter 7 requires passing the means test. New York's median income for a 1-person household is $66,973.

Creditor objections. In some conversions, creditors or the U.S. Trustee may object, particularly if there are allegations of bad faith.

Property acquired post-filing. Property acquired between the original filing and conversion becomes part of the estate and may be subject to liquidation in Chapter 7.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I convert my Chapter 13 to Chapter 7?

Yes. A debtor has an absolute right to convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. section 1307(a), as long as the case was not previously converted from Chapter 7. You must still qualify under the means test. The conversion fee is $25.

Can I convert my Chapter 7 to Chapter 13?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 706(a), a debtor may convert a Chapter 7 case to Chapter 13 at any time, as long as the case has not been previously converted. You must have regular income and debts within the Chapter 13 limits.

Why would I convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7?

Common reasons include: your income dropped and you can no longer afford plan payments, you lost your job, your non-exempt assets are minimal and Chapter 7 would be faster, or your Chapter 13 plan is failing.

What happens to my payments if I convert from 13 to 7?

Payments you already made to the Chapter 13 trustee are generally returned to you minus any amounts already distributed to creditors and trustee fees. Secured creditor payments that were already forwarded cannot be recovered.

Not Sure Which Chapter Is Right?

Compare the key differences and check your eligibility.

Chapter 7 vs 13
Free Discharge Screener

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in New York? - Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/cost.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in New York?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

$1,500-$6,000

Typical Attorney Fees

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,500 - $3,500Typical New York range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Total estimate$1,900 - $3,900

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$3,500 - $6,000Typical New York range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Total estimate$3,900 - $6,400Most paid through plan, not upfront

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

A major advantage of Chapter 13 is that attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment, with the rest paid through monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Legal Aid Society of New York - provides free bankruptcy representation for qualifying low-income individuals

Fee waiver - eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments - pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing - filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended (only 18.9% of S.D.N.Y. filers do this)

Reduced-fee credit counseling - many approved agencies offer reduced fees for low-income filers

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in New York?

The filing fee is $338. Attorney fees typically range from $1,500 - $3,500. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. Total: typically $1,900 - $3,900. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in New York?

The filing fee is $313 and attorney fees typically range from $3,500 - $6,000. Most costs can be paid through your repayment plan.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Legal Aid Society of New York may provide free representation.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, fees are typically included in your repayment plan.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?

## Bankruptcy by County - New York Metro Area
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/counties.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Counties

Bankruptcy by County - New York Metro Area

Your county determines which federal court handles your bankruptcy case and which state exemptions protect your property. Find your county below for specific information.

5 Counties in the New York Metro

New York County (Manhattan)

New York County (Manhattan) is the financial capital of the world and the most densely populated county in the United States. Bankruptcy cases are handled by the Southern District of New York, one of the busiest bankruptcy courts in the nation.

District: Southern District of New York (Manhattan Division)

Courthouse: One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

New York County (Manhattan) bankruptcy guide →

Kings County (Brooklyn)

Kings County (Brooklyn) is the most populous borough in New York City. Unlike Manhattan, Brooklyn falls under the Eastern District of New York - a different federal court district with its own courthouse and judges.

District: Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn Division)

Courthouse: 271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

Kings County (Brooklyn) bankruptcy guide →

Queens County

Queens County is the most ethnically diverse urban area in the world and the second-largest New York City borough by population. Cases are filed in the Eastern District of New York.

District: Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn Division)

Courthouse: 271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

Queens County bankruptcy guide →

Bronx County

Bronx County is the northernmost borough of New York City. Like Manhattan, it falls under the Southern District of New York. Bronx residents file at the Manhattan courthouse.

District: Southern District of New York (Manhattan Division)

Courthouse: One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

Bronx County bankruptcy guide →

Westchester County

Westchester County is directly north of New York City and includes affluent suburbs like Scarsdale, White Plains, and New Rochelle. It has its own division within the Southern District of New York.

District: Southern District of New York (White Plains Division)

Courthouse: 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

Westchester County bankruptcy guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which counties are in the New York bankruptcy metro area?

The New York metro area covers 5 counties: New York County (Manhattan), Kings County (Brooklyn), Queens County, Bronx County, Westchester County. Each county falls under a specific federal bankruptcy court district.

Does it matter which county I live in for bankruptcy?

Yes. Your county determines which federal district court handles your case, and potentially which state exemptions apply. Different districts may have different local rules, trustees, and dismissal rates.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Bronx County - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/counties/bronx-county.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Bronx County

Bankruptcy in Bronx County

Bronx County is the northernmost borough of New York City. Like Manhattan, it falls under the Southern District of New York. Bronx residents file at the Manhattan courthouse.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of New York (Manhattan Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Bronx County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

New York Exemptions in Bronx County

New York state or federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 (Bronx County). Vehicle: $4,825. Cash: $5,000. Personal property exemptions vary by category. Federal alternative: homestead $27,900 per person, vehicle $4,450, wildcard up to $15,425 combined.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Bronx County

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Southern District of New York (Manhattan Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which court handles Bronx County bankruptcy cases?

Bronx County cases are filed in the Southern District of New York, the same district as Manhattan. The courthouse is at One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004. Bronx residents travel to Lower Manhattan for hearings and the 341 meeting of creditors.

Is the Bronx in the same district as Manhattan or Brooklyn?

The Bronx is in the Southern District of New York, the same as Manhattan. Brooklyn and Queens are in the Eastern District. This means Bronx cases are handled by the same judges and follow the same local rules as Manhattan cases.

What is the median income for the means test in the Bronx?

The means test uses New York state median income figures adjusted for household size. For a household of four, the New York median is approximately $110,000. The means test also adjusts for local housing and transportation costs, which can benefit Bronx filers.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Kings County (Brooklyn) - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/counties/kings-county-brooklyn.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Kings County (Brooklyn)

Bankruptcy in Kings County (Brooklyn)

Kings County (Brooklyn) is the most populous borough in New York City. Unlike Manhattan, Brooklyn falls under the Eastern District of New York - a different federal court district with its own courthouse and judges.

Key Information

Federal District

Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Kings County (Brooklyn) are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

New York Exemptions in Kings County (Brooklyn)

New York state or federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 (Kings County). Vehicle: $4,825. Cash: $5,000. Personal property: varies. Federal alternative: homestead $27,900 per person, vehicle $4,450, wildcard $1,475 plus $13,950 unused homestead.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Kings County (Brooklyn)

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Brooklyn in a different bankruptcy district than Manhattan?

Yes. Kings County (Brooklyn) is in the Eastern District of New York, while Manhattan is in the Southern District. The Brooklyn bankruptcy courthouse is at 271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201. This means different judges and potentially different local practices.

What is the homestead exemption in Brooklyn?

The New York state homestead exemption for Kings County is $179,975. Alternatively, you can use federal exemptions with a homestead of $27,900 per person. Given Brooklyn's high real estate values, many homeowners have equity exceeding the state exemption.

Can I protect my co-op apartment in Brooklyn?

Co-op apartments can be protected under the homestead exemption if the co-op is your primary residence. The $179,975 state exemption or $27,900 federal exemption applies to the equity in your co-op shares. Consult a qualified attorney for co-op-specific issues.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Queens County
Bronx County

## Bankruptcy in New York County (Manhattan) - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/counties/new-york-county-manhattan.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » New York County (Manhattan)

Bankruptcy in New York County (Manhattan)

New York County (Manhattan) is the financial capital of the world and the most densely populated county in the United States. Bankruptcy cases are handled by the Southern District of New York, one of the busiest bankruptcy courts in the nation.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of New York (Manhattan Division)

All bankruptcy cases from New York County (Manhattan) are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

New York Exemptions in New York County (Manhattan)

New York allows a choice between state and federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975-$299,975 depending on county (New York County: $179,975). Vehicle: $4,825. Cash: $5,000. Personal property: varies by category. Federal exemptions available as alternative: homestead $27,900, vehicle $4,450, wildcard $1,475 plus $13,950 of unused homestead.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in New York County (Manhattan)

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Southern District of New York (Manhattan Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Manhattan cases?

New York County (Manhattan) cases are filed in the Southern District of New York. The bankruptcy courthouse is at One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004, in Lower Manhattan near Battery Park. This is one of the busiest bankruptcy courts in the country.

Can I choose between state and federal exemptions in Manhattan?

Yes. New York is one of the states that allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions. The state homestead exemption for New York County is $179,975. The federal homestead exemption is approximately $27,900 per person ($55,800 for a married couple). Most Manhattan residents with significant home equity benefit from the state exemption.

How does the high cost of living affect bankruptcy in Manhattan?

The means test adjusts for local housing and transportation costs, which benefits Manhattan filers. Higher allowed expenses can make it easier to qualify for Chapter 7. However, the homestead exemption of $179,975 may be insufficient to protect equity in Manhattan real estate.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Queens County

## Bankruptcy in Queens County - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/counties/queens-county.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Queens County

Bankruptcy in Queens County

Queens County is the most ethnically diverse urban area in the world and the second-largest New York City borough by population. Cases are filed in the Eastern District of New York.

Key Information

Federal District

Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Queens County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

New York Exemptions in Queens County

New York state or federal exemptions apply. State homestead: $179,975 (Queens County). Vehicle: $4,825. Cash: $5,000. Federal alternative available: homestead $27,900 per person, vehicle $4,450, wildcard $1,475 plus unused homestead up to $13,950.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Queens County

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Eastern District of New York (Brooklyn Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Queens County residents file bankruptcy?

Queens County cases are filed in the Eastern District of New York. The courthouse is at 271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201 - the same courthouse used for Brooklyn cases. Queens residents should plan for travel to the Brooklyn courthouse for their 341 meeting.

What exemptions apply to Queens County residents?

Queens County residents can choose between New York state exemptions (homestead: $179,975) or federal exemptions (homestead: $27,900 per person). The best choice depends on your specific assets. Many Queens homeowners benefit from the higher state homestead exemption.

Can I file bankruptcy on my small business in Queens?

If you are a sole proprietor, your business debts and personal debts are combined in a personal bankruptcy filing. Chapter 7 may require liquidating business assets, while Chapter 13 can allow you to continue operating while repaying debts. Consult a qualified attorney for business-specific guidance.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Westchester County - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/counties/westchester-county.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Westchester County

Bankruptcy in Westchester County

Westchester County is directly north of New York City and includes affluent suburbs like Scarsdale, White Plains, and New Rochelle. It has its own division within the Southern District of New York.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of New York (White Plains Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Westchester County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

New York Exemptions in Westchester County

New York state or federal exemptions. State homestead: $179,975 (Westchester County falls in the middle tier). Vehicle: $4,825. Cash: $5,000. Personal property: varies. Federal alternative: homestead $27,900 per person. Westchester's high home values often exceed the state exemption.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Westchester County

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Southern District of New York (White Plains Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Westchester County residents file bankruptcy?

Westchester County cases are filed in the Southern District of New York, White Plains Division. The courthouse is at 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601. This is more convenient for Westchester residents than traveling to Manhattan.

Is the homestead exemption sufficient for Westchester County homes?

The New York state homestead exemption of $179,975 may be insufficient for many Westchester County homes, where median home values are significantly higher. Homeowners with substantial equity should carefully consider Chapter 13, which allows you to keep your home regardless of equity.

Can I file Chapter 7 with a high income in Westchester?

The means test adjusts for local costs, and Westchester's high housing and transportation costs can increase allowed deductions. However, a high income does not automatically disqualify you - the test compares your income to the state median and subtracts allowed expenses. Many Westchester residents with above-median income still qualify.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in New York - Timeline and Steps
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/credit-after-bankruptcy.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Credit After Bankruptcy

Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in New York

Bankruptcy is not the end of your financial life - it is the beginning of a fresh start. Most people see significant credit improvement within 12-24 months of their discharge by following a disciplined rebuilding plan.

Credit Recovery Timeline

Immediately After Discharge

Check Your Credit Reports

Pull free reports from all three bureaus at AnnualCreditReport.com. Verify all discharged debts show a $0 balance. Dispute any errors - this is common and fixing them can boost your score quickly.

Month 1-3

Get a Secured Credit Card

Apply for a secured credit card with a $200-$500 deposit. Use it for small purchases (under 30% of the limit) and pay the full balance every month. Local New York credit unions like Municipal Credit Union of NYC and Bethpage Federal Credit Union are often more willing to work with recent filers than big banks.

Month 3-6

Add a Credit-Builder Loan

A credit-builder loan from a credit union deposits money into a savings account as you make payments. This adds an installment account to your credit mix and builds payment history.

Month 6-12

Score Begins Recovering

With consistent on-time payments and low utilization, most filers see scores in the 600-650 range within a year. This is enough for many auto loans, some apartments, and basic credit cards.

Year 1-2

Graduate to Unsecured Credit

After 12-18 months of responsible secured card use, apply for an unsecured card. Keep the secured card open for length of credit history.

Year 2-4

Major Purchases Become Possible

FHA mortgage loans are available 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge (1 year into Chapter 13 with court approval). Auto loans at reasonable rates. Scores often reach 680-720 range.

How Long Bankruptcy Stays on Your Credit Report

ChapterTime on Credit ReportTypical Score Recovery

Chapter 710 years from filing date650+ within 2 years

Chapter 137 years from filing date650+ within 1-2 years

Important: The impact diminishes long before the record drops off. After 2-3 years of active rebuilding, most lenders focus on your recent payment history rather than the bankruptcy itself.

Buying a Home After Bankruptcy in New York

New York's housing market is competitive, so rebuilding credit quickly matters. Here are the waiting periods for mortgage loans:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years after discharge1 year into plan (court approval)

VA2 years after discharge1 year into plan

USDA3 years after discharge1 year into plan

Conventional4 years after discharge2 years after discharge

With New York homestead exemptions protecting $179,975-$215,550 in equity, many homeowners keep their homes through bankruptcy and continue building equity.

New York Credit Unions for Rebuilding

Credit unions are often more willing to work with recent bankruptcy filers than large banks. These New York credit unions offer secured cards, credit-builder loans, and second-chance checking accounts:

Municipal Credit Union of NYC - Serves NYC residents and employees. Known for working with members rebuilding credit.

Bethpage Federal Credit Union - Long Island based, open to all New York residents. Offers secured credit cards and credit-builder programs.

Lower East Side People's FCU - Community-focused, mission-driven lending in Manhattan.

Brooklyn Cooperative FCU - Serves Brooklyn residents with financial inclusion programs.

Practical Rebuilding Tips

Pay everything on time. Payment history is 35% of your credit score. Set up autopay for minimums on every account.

Keep utilization below 30%. On a $500 secured card, never carry a balance above $150.

Do not close old accounts that survived bankruptcy. Length of credit history matters.

Limit hard inquiries. Only apply for new credit when you need it. Each application temporarily lowers your score.

Monitor your credit. Use free services like Credit Karma or your bank's score tracker. Dispute inaccurate items promptly.

Build an emergency fund. Even $500-$1,000 prevents the debt spiral that may have led to bankruptcy.

Become an authorized user. A trusted family member can add you to their card to boost your credit history.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report?

Chapter 7 stays for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. The actual impact diminishes significantly after 2-3 years of active rebuilding. Full rebuilding guide.

Can I get a credit card after bankruptcy?

Yes. Many people qualify for a secured credit card immediately after discharge. A secured card requires a cash deposit ($200-$500) as your credit limit. After 12-18 months of responsible use, you may qualify for an unsecured card.

How soon can I buy a house after bankruptcy in New York?

FHA loans are available 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge. Conventional loans require 4 years. New York's homestead exemption protects $179,975-$215,550 in equity, so many homeowners keep their homes through bankruptcy.

Can I get a car loan after bankruptcy?

Yes, often immediately after discharge. Initial rates are higher (10-20%), but they improve as credit recovers. After 2-3 years of rebuilding, many people qualify for rates close to prime.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your House
Keep Your Car
Bankruptcy Cost

## Discharging Credit Card Debt in New York Bankruptcy
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/credit-card-debt.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Discharging Credit Card Debt in New York Bankruptcy

Discharging Credit Card Debt in New York Bankruptcy

Credit card debt is one of the most common reasons people file bankruptcy. It is generally dischargeable, but there are important exceptions involving recent charges and fraud.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 vs Chapter 13 for Credit Card Debt

Chapter 7

Credit card debt eliminated in 3-4 months

No repayment required

Must pass the means test (median income $66,973 for 1 person in NY)

Non-exempt assets may be liquidated (but most filers keep everything)

Filing fee: $338

Chapter 13

Pay a portion of credit card debt over 3-5 years

Remaining balance discharged at end of plan

No means test income limit - available to all

Keep all your property

Filing fee: $313

Unsecured creditors often receive 0-10% of what they are owed

Exceptions: When Credit Card Debt Is NOT Dischargeable

Recent Luxury Purchases (90-Day Rule)

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), charges for luxury goods or services to a single creditor totaling more than $800 within 90 days of filing are presumed nondischargeable. "Luxury" generally means anything that is not reasonably necessary for support - groceries and utilities are fine, but a new TV or vacation charges could be challenged.

Recent Cash Advances (70-Day Rule)

Cash advances totaling more than $1,100 within 70 days of filing are also presumed nondischargeable. This includes convenience checks and balance transfers that are treated as cash advances.

Fraud

If a creditor can prove you incurred the debt through fraud, false pretenses, or a false written statement (like lying on a credit application), the debt may not be dischargeable under section 523(a)(2)(A). The creditor must file an adversary proceeding and prove their case - the debt is not automatically excluded.

New York Garnishment and Collection

Before filing, New York credit card holders face these collection tools:

Wage garnishment: Creditors with a judgment can garnish the lesser of 10% of gross wages or 25% of disposable earnings. The automatic stay stops this immediately.

Bank account restraint: New York allows creditors to freeze bank accounts with a restraining notice. However, accounts with less than $3,600 (240 times the state minimum wage) are exempt from restraint.

Statute of limitations: New York has a 6-year statute of limitations on credit card debt. After 6 years without payment or acknowledgment, the debt is time-barred from lawsuit (but not from collection calls).

Practical Tips

Stop using credit cards as soon as you decide to file. Charges made after the decision to file can be challenged as fraudulent.

Wait 90 days after your last significant credit card purchase before filing, if possible.

Do not pay preferred creditors. Paying one credit card but not others in the 90 days before filing can be clawed back by the trustee as a preferential transfer.

List all credit cards on your schedules, even those with zero balances. Failure to list a debt can prevent its discharge.

Keep statements. Your last 6 months of credit card statements will be reviewed during the 341 meeting.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge credit card debt in bankruptcy?

Yes. Credit card debt is unsecured debt and is generally dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the debt is eliminated in 3-4 months. In Chapter 13, you pay a portion over 3-5 years and the remainder is discharged.

Are there exceptions for recent credit card charges?

Yes. Luxury goods or services charged to a single creditor totaling more than $800 within 90 days of filing are presumed nondischargeable. Cash advances totaling more than $1,100 within 70 days of filing are also presumed nondischargeable. The creditor must object to prevent discharge.

What if a creditor claims fraud?

A creditor can file an adversary proceeding arguing that the debt was obtained by fraud or false pretenses under section 523(a)(2). They must prove you had no intent to repay when you made the charges. This is relatively rare and the creditor bears the burden of proof.

How much credit card debt do I need to file?

There is no minimum amount of debt required to file bankruptcy. However, because of filing costs ($338 for Chapter 7, plus attorney fees typically $1,000-$2,000 in New York), bankruptcy usually makes practical sense when credit card debt exceeds $10,000.

Will bankruptcy stop credit card collection calls and lawsuits?

Yes. The automatic stay immediately stops all collection calls, letters, lawsuits, and wage garnishments. In New York, creditors can garnish 10% of gross or 25% of disposable wages (whichever is less), so the stay provides immediate relief.

Check Your Discharge Eligibility

Prior filings can affect your ability to discharge debt. Use our free screener to check.

Free Discharge Screener
Chapter 7 vs 13

## Using Credit Cards Before Filing Bankruptcy
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/credit-cards-before-filing.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Using Credit Cards Before Filing Bankruptcy

Using Credit Cards Before Filing Bankruptcy

A free guide for New York residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

Using credit cards when you know you will file bankruptcy can be considered fraud. The Bankruptcy Code presumes fraud for luxury goods purchases over $725 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,000 within 70 days of filing. Best practice: stop using all credit cards immediately once you decide to file. The creditor can challenge discharge of those specific charges.

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Using Credit Cards Before Filing Bankruptcy

Using credit cards when you know you will file bankruptcy can be considered fraud. The Bankruptcy Code presumes fraud for luxury goods purchases over $725 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,000 within 70 days of filing. Best practice: stop using all credit cards immediately once you decide to file. The creditor can challenge discharge of those specific charges.

Does this apply in New York?

Yes. This applies to S.D.N.Y.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Types of Debt in Bankruptcy - What Can and Cannot Be Discharged
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/debt-types.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Debt Types

Types of Debt in Bankruptcy

Not all debts are treated equally in bankruptcy. Understanding which debts can be discharged (eliminated) and which survive is critical to deciding whether bankruptcy is right for you.

Dischargeable Debts (Can Be Eliminated)

Debt TypeDischargeable?Notes

Credit card debtYesFully dischargeable. Recent luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days may be challenged.

Medical billsYesFully dischargeable. No minimum or maximum.

Personal loansYesIncluding payday loans, installment loans, signature loans.

Old utility billsYesPast-due electric, gas, water, phone bills.

Collection accountsYesEven debts that have been sold to collection agencies.

Civil judgmentsUsuallyMost money judgments are dischargeable. Fraud judgments are not.

Deficiency balancesYesFrom repossessions or foreclosures.

Lease obligationsYesEarly termination penalties on apartment or car leases.

Some older taxesConditionalMust meet 3-year/2-year/240-day rules. Details.

Non-Dischargeable Debts (Survive Bankruptcy)

Debt TypeDischargeable?Notes

Student loansRarelyMust prove undue hardship (Brunner test in 2nd Circuit).

Child supportNoDomestic support obligations are never dischargeable.

Alimony/spousal supportNoDomestic support obligations are never dischargeable.

Recent taxesNoTaxes that do not meet the 3-year/2-year/240-day rules. Details.

Fraud debtsNoDebts from fraud, false pretenses, or false financial statements.

Willful injuryNoDebts from intentional harm to another person or property.

DUI-related debtsNoDeath or personal injury caused by intoxicated driving.

Government finesNoCriminal fines, penalties, and court-ordered restitution.

Certain HOA feesPartialPost-petition HOA fees are not dischargeable if you keep the property.

The full list of non-dischargeable debts is found in 11 U.S.C. section 523(a).

Secured vs. Unsecured Debt

Secured Debt

Backed by collateral - the lender can take the property if you default.

Mortgages (keep your house)

Car loans (keep your car)

Home equity loans/HELOCs

In bankruptcy, you choose to keep paying (reaffirm), surrender the collateral, or address through a Chapter 13 plan.

Unsecured Debt

No collateral - the creditor's only option is to sue for a money judgment.

Credit cards

Medical bills

Personal loans

Most unsecured debt is eliminated in bankruptcy through the discharge.

Priority Debts

Some unsecured debts are classified as "priority" under 11 U.S.C. section 507. These must be paid in full in Chapter 13 and survive Chapter 7 discharge:

Domestic support obligations (child support, alimony) - highest priority

Administrative expenses (trustee fees, attorney fees approved by the court)

Certain tax debts - priority taxes from the last 3 years

Employee wages - up to $15,150 per employee earned within 180 days pre-filing

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts can be eliminated in bankruptcy?

Most unsecured debts: credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, payday loans, old utility bills, deficiency balances, and certain older taxes. Chapter 7 eliminates them in 3-4 months.

What debts cannot be discharged?

Student loans (unless undue hardship), child support, alimony, recent taxes, fraud debts, DUI injury debts, and government fines. Full list: 11 U.S.C. section 523(a).

Is credit card debt dischargeable?

Yes. Credit card debt is fully dischargeable. Recent luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days or cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days may be challenged but are still often discharged.

What is the difference between secured and unsecured debt?

Secured debt has collateral (mortgage, car loan). Unsecured has none (credit cards, medical bills). In bankruptcy, most unsecured debt is discharged while secured debt requires you to keep paying, surrender, or restructure through a plan.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Related Topics

Medical Debt
Student Loans
Tax Debt
Wage Garnishment
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions

## Bankruptcy Disqualifications in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/disqualifications.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Bankruptcy Disqualifications in New York

Bankruptcy Disqualifications in New York

Not everyone can file every chapter of bankruptcy. Here are the barriers to filing and how they apply to New York residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Discharge Waiting Periods

Prior CaseNew CaseWait for DischargeStatute

Chapter 7Chapter 78 years727(a)(8)

Chapter 7Chapter 134 years1328(f)(1)

Chapter 13Chapter 76 years*727(a)(9)

Chapter 13Chapter 132 years1328(f)(2)

*6-year bar is waived if the prior Chapter 13 paid 100% of unsecured claims or paid at least 70% in a good-faith, best-effort plan.

Use the 1328(f) screener to check your specific eligibility.

Filing Bars

180-Day Bar (Section 109(g))

You cannot file any bankruptcy case if a prior case was dismissed within the past 180 days because:

You willfully failed to appear before the court or comply with court orders, OR

You requested voluntary dismissal after a creditor filed a motion for relief from the automatic stay

Automatic Stay Limitations

One prior dismissal in past year: Automatic stay expires after 30 days unless you file a motion to extend it - section 362(c)(3)

Two or more dismissals in past year: No automatic stay at all unless you get a court order - section 362(c)(4)

Credit Counseling Requirement

You must complete an approved credit counseling course within 180 days before filing. No exceptions (except in declared disaster areas or where no approved agency is available). Courses are available online and typically cost $20-50.

Chapter-Specific Disqualifications

Chapter 7

Means test failure - income too high after allowed deductions. New York median: $66,973 (1 person). See Chapter 7 eligibility.

Stockbrokers and commodity brokers - cannot use Chapter 7; must use SIPA liquidation

Prior Chapter 7 discharge within 8 years

Chapter 13

No regular income - must have income sufficient to fund a plan

Debt exceeds $2,750,000 (combined secured + unsecured)

Not an individual - businesses, corporations, and LLCs cannot file Chapter 13

Frequently Asked Questions

What disqualifies me from Chapter 7?

Failing the means test, a prior Chapter 7 discharge within 8 years, a prior Chapter 13 discharge within 6 years, dismissal for abuse within 180 days, or not completing credit counseling.

Can I file again after a dismissal?

Yes, with restrictions. The 180-day bar applies if dismissed for abuse. Even outside that bar, prior dismissals limit your automatic stay protection. Use the 1328(f) screener.

Can I file without a discharge?

Yes. You can file a new case even within the waiting periods - you just will not receive a discharge. This is sometimes done strategically (e.g., "Chapter 20" - filing Chapter 13 after Chapter 7 to restructure secured debts).

What if I cannot qualify for any chapter?

This is extremely rare. If Chapter 7 and 13 are both unavailable, Chapter 11 has no means test and no debt limits for individuals. It is more expensive but always available.

Free Discharge Screener
File Again Guide

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home1328(f) ScreenerCh. 7 vs 13Automatic Stay341 MeetingMeans TestCost GuideDismissed?File Again?How to File

## Find Your Bankruptcy District - New York Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/district-lookup.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Find Your Bankruptcy District - New York Bankruptcy Guide›Find Your Bankruptcy District

Find Your Bankruptcy District

Enter your zip code to find which federal bankruptcy court handles cases in your area of the New York City metro.

Zip Code Lookup

Look Up

New York City Metro Bankruptcy Districts

Southern District of New York (S.D.N.Y.)

Covers Manhattan, the Bronx, and counties north of NYC including Westchester, Rockland, Putnam, Orange, Dutchess, and Sullivan. Zip codes typically 100xx-104xx (NYC) and 105xx-109xx (suburban).

Courthouse: One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

White Plains: 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

Eastern District of New York (E.D.N.Y.)

Covers Brooklyn, Queens, Staten Island, Nassau County, and Suffolk County (all of Long Island). Zip codes typically 110xx-119xx.

Courthouse: 271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201

Central Islip: 290 Federal Plaza, Central Islip, NY 11722

Northern District of New York (N.D.N.Y.)

Covers upstate New York including Albany, Syracuse, Binghamton, and Utica. Zip codes typically 120xx-139xx.

Courthouse: 10 Broad St, Utica, NY 13501

Western District of New York (W.D.N.Y.)

Covers Buffalo, Rochester, and western New York. Zip codes typically 140xx-149xx.

Courthouse: 2 Niagara Square, Buffalo, NY 14202

District of New Jersey (D.N.J.)

Many NYC-area commuters live in New Jersey. If your zip code starts with 07xxx-08xxx, you file in New Jersey, not New York.

Courthouse: 50 Walnut St, Newark, NJ 07102

New Jersey district lookup →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy district covers Manhattan?

Manhattan is in the Southern District of New York (S.D.N.Y.). The courthouse is at One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004. S.D.N.Y. also covers the Bronx, Westchester, Rockland, Putnam, Orange, Dutchess, and Sullivan counties. Zip codes 100xx-104xx, 105xx-109xx.

Which district covers Brooklyn and Queens?

Brooklyn, Queens, Staten Island, and Long Island are in the Eastern District of New York (E.D.N.Y.). The courthouse is at 271-C Cadman Plaza East, Brooklyn, NY 11201. E.D.N.Y. covers Kings, Queens, Richmond, Nassau, and Suffolk counties. Zip codes 110xx-119xx.

I live in New Jersey but work in New York. Where do I file?

You file where you live, not where you work. If you live in New Jersey, you file in the District of New Jersey. See our Newark Bankruptcy Guide for New Jersey district information.

Other City Bankruptcy Guides

Houston
Miami

## Divorce and Bankruptcy in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/divorce-and-bankruptcy.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Divorce and Bankruptcy in New York

Divorce and Bankruptcy in New York

Divorce and bankruptcy often happen together. The order in which you file matters, and certain debts from divorce cannot be discharged. Here is how these two processes interact for New York residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Order: Bankruptcy Before or After Divorce?

Bankruptcy First

Eliminates joint debts before property division, simplifying the divorce

You can file a joint petition and split the cost (one filing fee instead of two)

Both spouses can use their full New York exemptions

Faster resolution - Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months

Requires cooperation between spouses on the petition

Divorce First

Single-filer means test uses only your income (median $66,973 for 1 person in NY)

May qualify for Chapter 7 on single income when household income was too high

No need to cooperate with ex-spouse on bankruptcy paperwork

Divorce decree may assign specific debts, clarifying what you owe

Higher total cost (two separate filings if both need to file)

What Bankruptcy Cannot Discharge

Domestic Support Obligations (DSOs)

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(5), the following debts survive bankruptcy and cannot be discharged in any chapter:

Child support (current and past-due)

Alimony / spousal maintenance

Any debt designated as a domestic support obligation

The automatic stay does not stop collection of DSOs. Child support enforcement, wage withholding for support, and paternity/custody proceedings all continue during bankruptcy.

Property Settlement Debts

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(15), debts owed to a spouse or former spouse from a divorce decree, separation agreement, or property settlement are not dischargeable in Chapter 7. However, they may be dischargeable in Chapter 13 - this is one reason some filers choose Chapter 13 after divorce.

Joint Debts After Divorce

One of the most common problems: your divorce decree says your ex-spouse is responsible for a joint credit card or mortgage, but they file bankruptcy and discharge it. The creditor can still come after you because the divorce decree is between you and your spouse - the creditor was not a party to it.

Options in this situation:

File your own bankruptcy to discharge the debt

Go back to family court to modify the divorce decree

Negotiate directly with the creditor

New York is an equitable distribution state, meaning property and debts are divided fairly but not necessarily 50/50. This can create complex situations when bankruptcy is added to the mix.

New York-Specific Considerations

Equitable distribution. New York divides marital property equitably, not equally. The bankruptcy court and divorce court may have overlapping jurisdiction over the same assets.

Homestead exemption. The New York homestead exemption ($179,975--$215,550 by county) protects equity in your primary residence. If you and your spouse own a home jointly, the timing of bankruptcy and divorce affects how this exemption applies.

State vs. federal exemptions. New York lets you choose between state and federal exemptions. A bankruptcy attorney can help determine which set protects more of your property in a divorce situation.

Mandatory settlement conference. New York requires a settlement conference in foreclosure cases. If your home is at risk in both a divorce and a bankruptcy, this process may run in parallel.

Frequently Asked Questions

Should I file bankruptcy before or after divorce?

It depends on your situation. Filing before divorce can simplify property division by eliminating joint debts, and you can file jointly to save on fees. Filing after divorce may be better if you need to use single-filer income limits for the means test or if your divorce settlement assigns debts that you want to discharge.

Can bankruptcy discharge alimony or child support?

No. Alimony and child support are classified as domestic support obligations (DSOs) under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(5) and cannot be discharged in any chapter of bankruptcy. These obligations survive bankruptcy and collection of DSOs is not stopped by the automatic stay.

What happens to joint debt after divorce if my ex files bankruptcy?

If your divorce decree assigns a joint debt to your ex-spouse and they file bankruptcy, you are still liable to the creditor for the full amount. The creditor was not a party to your divorce and the divorce decree does not change the original contract. You may need to file your own bankruptcy to address this.

Can I file joint bankruptcy during a pending divorce?

Yes, you can file a joint bankruptcy petition while a divorce is pending. This can be an efficient way to eliminate joint debts before the divorce is finalized. However, both parties must cooperate on the petition and schedules.

Does bankruptcy affect property division in divorce?

The automatic stay can pause divorce proceedings related to property division while the bankruptcy case is open. However, family court proceedings regarding custody, visitation, child support, and domestic violence continue. New York is an equitable distribution state.

Understand Your Options

Check whether prior filings affect your eligibility before making decisions about timing.

## Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/emergency-bankruptcy.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›New York Bankruptcy Guide›Emergency Filing

Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in New York

When a foreclosure sale, wage garnishment, or vehicle repossession is hours or days away, an emergency bankruptcy filing can stop it immediately. Here is how it works in the Southern District of New York.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Emergency Filing Works

An emergency (or "bare-bones") bankruptcy filing lets you activate the automatic stay immediately by filing just a minimal petition. You then have 14 days to complete the rest of your paperwork.

1

File the Petition

File Official Form 101, a creditor matrix (list of creditors with addresses), and your credit counseling certificate. This can be done electronically through the S.D.N.Y. system.

2

Stay Activates

The automatic stay takes effect the instant the petition is filed. Creditors must stop all collection, foreclosure, and garnishment activity.

3

Complete in 14 Days

File your full schedules, statement of financial affairs, means test, and all remaining documents within 14 days or the case may be dismissed.

When You Might Need an Emergency Filing

Foreclosure sale is imminent. New York uses judicial foreclosure (12-18 months typical), but once a sale date is set, you may need to act fast. Filing before the sale stops it cold.

Wage garnishment just started. New York allows garnishment of 10% of gross or 25% of disposable wages (whichever is less). Filing stops the garnishment immediately.

Vehicle repossession is threatened. If a repo agent is coming, filing activates the stay and makes repossession a violation of federal law.

Bank account levy. A creditor with a judgment can freeze your bank account in New York. An emergency filing stops this.

Utility shutoff. Filing gives you 20 days of protection from utility disconnection.

What You Need to File

Day 1 (Emergency)

Voluntary Petition (Official Form 101)

Creditor matrix (names and addresses)

Credit counseling certificate

Filing fee ($338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13) or fee waiver application

Social Security number statement

Within 14 Days

Schedules A/B through J (all assets, debts, income, expenses)

Statement of Financial Affairs

Means Test (Chapter 7) or Disposable Income (Chapter 13)

Tax returns (last 4 years if requested by trustee)

Pay stubs for the last 60 days

Chapter 13 plan (if filing Chapter 13)

New York-Specific Considerations

S.D.N.Y. Courthouse Locations

The Southern District of New York has courthouses at One Bowling Green, Manhattan and 300 Quarropas Street, White Plains. Electronic filing is available 24/7 through the court's CM/ECF system.

New York Exemptions Still Apply

Even in an emergency filing, your New York exemptions protect your property. New York allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions. The state homestead exemption ranges from $179,975 to $215,550 depending on your county, and the vehicle exemption is $4,825.

Repeat Filer Warning

If you had a bankruptcy case dismissed within the past year, the automatic stay from an emergency filing only lasts 30 days (11 U.S.C. section 362(c)(3)). Two or more prior dismissals means no stay at all without a court order. Check your eligibility with the 1328(f) screener.

Risks of Emergency Filing

Dismissal risk. If you miss the 14-day deadline for remaining documents, your case is dismissed and creditors can resume collection.

Incomplete strategy. Rushing to file means less time to choose the right chapter (7 vs. 13) and plan your exemptions.

Refiling restrictions. A dismissed case can trigger the 30-day stay limitation if you need to file again within a year.

Creditor matrix errors. Leaving creditors off the emergency matrix means they will not receive notice of the stay.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is an emergency bankruptcy filing?

An emergency bankruptcy filing (also called a bare-bones or skeleton petition) is when you file just the petition and a few required documents to get an immediate case number and activate the automatic stay. You then have 14 days to file your remaining schedules and documents. This is used when you need to stop a foreclosure sale, repossession, or wage garnishment immediately.

How fast can I file emergency bankruptcy in New York?

An emergency bankruptcy can be filed the same day you decide to proceed. The S.D.N.Y. courthouse accepts filings electronically, and the automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed. If a foreclosure sale is scheduled for tomorrow, filing today will stop it.

What documents do I need for an emergency filing?

At minimum you need the petition (Official Form 101), a list of creditors with addresses (the creditor matrix), and your credit counseling certificate. You have 14 days after filing to submit your full schedules, means test, and other required documents.

What happens if I do not complete the filing within 14 days?

If you fail to file the remaining schedules and documents within 14 days, the court will dismiss your case. This means the automatic stay goes away and creditors can resume collection actions. You may also face restrictions on refiling.

Can I stop a foreclosure sale with an emergency filing?

Yes. The automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362 halts all foreclosure proceedings the instant the petition is filed. New York uses judicial foreclosure with a mandatory settlement conference, which takes 12-18 months, so you often have time to plan. But if a sale date is imminent, an emergency filing can stop it.

Check Your Filing Eligibility

Use our free screener to check whether prior filings affect your eligibility or stay protection.

## Bankruptcy and Employment: Can You Get Fired?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/employment.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Employment: Can You Get Fired?

Bankruptcy and Employment: Can You Get Fired?

A free guide for New York residents. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 525(a)) prohibits government employers from terminating, refusing to hire, or discriminating against you because of a bankruptcy filing. For private employers, the protection is more limited - they generally cannot fire you for filing, but some courts allow them to consider bankruptcy in hiring decisions. State laws vary and may provide additional protection.

In New York: New York has broad employment discrimination protections. NYC Human Rights Law provides additional coverage.

Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Bankruptcy and Employment: Can You Get Fired?

Federal law (11 U.S.C. Section 525(a)) prohibits government employers from terminating, refusing to hire, or discriminating against you because of a bankruptcy filing. For private employers, the protection is more limited - they generally cannot fire you for filing, but some courts allow them to consider bankruptcy in hiring decisions. State laws vary and may provide additional protection.

How does this work in New York?

New York has broad employment discrimination protections. NYC Human Rights Law provides additional coverage. Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

Where do I file?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Can I Keep My Engagement Ring in Bankruptcy?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/engagement-ring.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Can I Keep My Engagement Ring in Bankruptcy?

Can I Keep My Engagement Ring in Bankruptcy?

A free guide for New York residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

Whether you can keep your engagement ring depends on your state's jewelry exemption. Exemption amounts vary wildly - from $150 in Arizona to unlimited in Texas. If your ring's value exceeds the exemption, the trustee could potentially sell it (though this is rare in practice for modest rings). Many people protect rings using wildcard exemptions.

In New York: NY state: $1,000 jewelry. Federal alternative: $1,875 + wildcard.

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I Keep My Engagement Ring in Bankruptcy?

Whether you can keep your engagement ring depends on your state's jewelry exemption. Exemption amounts vary wildly - from $150 in Arizona to unlimited in Texas. If your ring's value exceeds the exemption, the trustee could potentially sell it (though this is rare in practice for modest rings). Many people protect rings using wildcard exemptions.

Does this apply in New York?

Yes. This applies to S.D.N.Y.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/eviction.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction?

Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction?

A free guide for New York residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

The automatic stay under Section 362 temporarily halts most eviction proceedings. However, Section 362(b)(22) limits protection if your landlord already has a judgment for possession. In Chapter 13, you may be able to cure rent arrears through your plan. Timing matters - file before the judgment if possible.

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction?

The automatic stay under Section 362 temporarily halts most eviction proceedings. However, Section 362(b)(22) limits protection if your landlord already has a judgment for possession. In Chapter 13, you may be able to cure rent arrears through your plan. Timing matters - file before the judgment if possible.

Does this apply in New York?

Yes. This applies to S.D.N.Y.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## New York Bankruptcy Exemptions - What You Can Keep
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Exemptions

New York Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding them is critical - they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

New York gives you the choice between state and federal exemptions. Many filers choose state exemptions because of the higher homestead protection, but federal exemptions offer a more generous wildcard.

New York Exemptions - Full List

Homestead - $179,975 - $215,550 (varies by county)

Protects up to $179,975 - $215,550 (varies by county) of equity in your primary residence. Equity is your home's current market value minus what you owe on the mortgage.

N.Y. CPLR 5206; Debtor & Creditor Law 282

Motor Vehicle - $4,825

Protects up to $4,825 of equity in one motor vehicle. If you owe more than the car is worth (you are "underwater"), there is no equity to protect and the exemption is not needed.

N.Y. CPLR 5205(a)(8); Debtor & Creditor Law 282

Other New York Exemptions

PropertyAmount

Household goods, furnishings, appliancesNo per-item limit

Tools of trade, books, equipment$3,000

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)Unlimited

Social Security benefitsUnlimited

Unemployment benefitsUnlimited

Workers' compensationUnlimited

Public assistance benefitsUnlimited

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the New York homestead exemption?

New York's homestead exemption protects up to $179,975 - $215,550 (varies by county) of equity in your primary residence. Equity is the difference between your home's value and what you owe on the mortgage.

How much vehicle equity can I protect in New York?

New York's motor vehicle exemption is $4,825. If your car equity is below that amount, you can keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you keep your car regardless. Learn about Chapter 13.

Are retirement accounts protected in New York?

Yes. Retirement accounts including IRAs, 401(k)s, and pensions are fully exempt. Social Security benefits are also fully protected under federal law (42 U.S.C. 407).

## New York Bankruptcy FAQ - 15 Common Questions Answered
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/faq.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Faq

Frequently Asked Questions

Answers to the 15 most common questions from New York residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in New York?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,500 - $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,500 - $6,000 for Chapter 13. Full cost breakdown.

What is the means test?

The means test compares your household income to the New York median. If below, you qualify for Chapter 7 automatically.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. Use the 1328(f) screener to check. Learn more.

Property and Assets

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy?

New York's homestead exemption protects up to $179,975 - $215,550 (varies by county) of home equity. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on missed payments. See all exemptions.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy?

New York protects $4,825 in vehicle equity. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments. Exemption details.

What happens to my tax refund in bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, your tax refund may be an asset. In Chapter 13, you may need to turn it over to the trustee. Timing matters - discuss with your attorney.

The Process

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months. Chapter 13 requires 3-5 years. Full timeline.

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes. Full guide.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay takes effect immediately upon filing. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay stops garnishments immediately. Learn more.

Types of Debt

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

Will bankruptcy eliminate my student loans?

Generally no, except in cases of undue hardship. Learn more.

Can creditors still call me after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection activity.

Life After Bankruptcy

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years; Chapter 13 for 7 years. Many filers see recovery within 1-2 years. Rebuilding credit.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster; Chapter 13 lets you keep property and catch up on debts. Compare them.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## How to Find Out if Someone Filed Bankruptcy
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/find-bankruptcy-filing.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›How to Find Out if Someone Filed Bankruptcy

How to Find Out if Someone Filed Bankruptcy

A free guide for New York residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

All bankruptcy filings are public record. Search PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) at pacer.gov for $0.10 per page. Free alternatives include RECAP (courtlistener.com/recap/) which mirrors many PACER documents. You can also call the bankruptcy court clerk directly or visit in person. Our free screener at 1328f.com can check discharge eligibility.

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

How to Find Out if Someone Filed Bankruptcy

All bankruptcy filings are public record. Search PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) at pacer.gov for $0.10 per page. Free alternatives include RECAP (courtlistener.com/recap/) which mirrors many PACER documents. You can also call the bankruptcy court clerk directly or visit in person. Our free screener at 1328f.com can check discharge eligibility.

Does this apply in New York?

Yes. This applies to S.D.N.Y.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Foreclosure and Bankruptcy in New York - Stop Foreclosure, Save Your Home
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/foreclosure.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Foreclosure

Foreclosure and Bankruptcy in New York

New York uses judicial foreclosure, which typically takes 12-18 months. Filing bankruptcy stops foreclosure immediately and can give you time to catch up on payments or negotiate alternatives.

New York Foreclosure Process

New York is a judicial foreclosure state, meaning the lender must file a lawsuit in state court. This provides significant protections and time for homeowners:

Day 1-90

Pre-Foreclosure Notice

Lender sends a 90-day pre-foreclosure notice under RPAPL section 1304. This notice must include information about housing counseling services.

Month 3-6

Complaint Filed

Lender files a foreclosure complaint in New York Supreme Court. You have 20-30 days to file an answer.

Month 6-12

Mandatory Settlement Conference

Under CPLR 3408, the court holds mandatory settlement conferences to explore loan modification and other alternatives.

Month 12-18+

Judgment and Sale

If no resolution, the court enters a judgment of foreclosure and sale. A referee conducts the auction. The entire process often takes 18+ months in New York.

How Bankruptcy Stops Foreclosure

Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362, which immediately halts the foreclosure process at whatever stage it is in:

Before the complaint: The lender cannot file the foreclosure lawsuit.

During litigation: The lawsuit is paused.

After judgment: The scheduled sale is stopped.

Day of sale: Even on the day of the auction, filing stops the sale (though timing is critical).

Chapter 13: Save Your Home

Chapter 13 is the most powerful tool for saving a home from foreclosure:

Cure arrears over 3-5 years: Missed mortgage payments are spread over the plan period. You pay a portion of the arrears each month in addition to your regular mortgage payment.

Regular payments continue: You must stay current on ongoing mortgage payments throughout the plan.

Strip second liens: If your home is worth less than your first mortgage, a second mortgage or HELOC can be classified as unsecured and potentially discharged.

Modify other debts: Credit cards, medical bills, and other unsecured debts are reduced, freeing income for mortgage payments.

Chapter 7 and Foreclosure

Chapter 7 provides temporary relief but does not permanently solve foreclosure:

The automatic stay temporarily stops foreclosure (3-4 months).

Personal liability for the mortgage debt can be discharged (no deficiency judgment).

But Chapter 7 does not provide a mechanism to catch up on missed payments.

After the stay lifts, the lender can resume foreclosure.

Chapter 7 is useful if you plan to surrender the home and want to eliminate the mortgage debt without a deficiency balance, or if you need a few months of breathing room while making other arrangements.

Deficiency Judgments in New York

If a foreclosure sale does not cover the full mortgage balance, the lender can seek a deficiency judgment in New York (RPAPL section 1371). The lender must file within 90 days of the sale. Bankruptcy can eliminate this deficiency:

Chapter 7: The deficiency is discharged as unsecured debt.

Chapter 13: The deficiency is treated as general unsecured debt in the plan, typically paid pennies on the dollar.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop foreclosure in New York?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy immediately stops foreclosure through the automatic stay. Chapter 13 lets you catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years while keeping your home.

How long does foreclosure take in New York?

New York uses judicial foreclosure, typically taking 12-18+ months. Mandatory settlement conferences and court backlogs often extend the timeline further.

Can I save my home with Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 allows you to cure mortgage arrears over 3-5 years while making regular monthly payments going forward. The automatic stay protects your home during the entire plan. Learn more.

What about the deficiency if my home sells for less than I owe?

New York allows deficiency judgments (lender must file within 90 days of sale). Bankruptcy can eliminate this deficiency in Chapter 7 or address it as unsecured debt in Chapter 13.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener
