## Bankruptcy Glossary - New York Terms
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/glossary.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Bankruptcy Glossary - New York Terms

Bankruptcy Glossary

Plain-English definitions of bankruptcy terms with New York-specific context where relevant.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

A-D

Adversary Proceeding

A lawsuit within a bankruptcy case. Common examples include challenges to dischargeability of specific debts (fraud, student loans) and preference actions by the trustee.

Automatic Stay

A federal court order under 11 U.S.C. section 362 that takes effect the instant you file bankruptcy. It stops most collection actions including wage garnishment, foreclosure, repossession, lawsuits, and creditor calls. See Automatic Stay in New York.

Bankruptcy Estate

All of your property and assets as of the filing date. The trustee administers the estate. Property protected by exemptions is removed from the estate.

Chapter 7

Liquidation bankruptcy. A trustee sells non-exempt assets to pay creditors, and remaining qualifying debts are discharged. Takes 3-4 months. Requires passing the means test. See Chapter 7 in New York.

Chapter 13

Reorganization for individuals with regular income. You keep your property and repay debts over a 3-5 year plan. See Chapter 13 in New York.

Co-Debtor Stay

In Chapter 13, the stay extends to co-signers on consumer debts (section 1301). Creditors cannot collect from your co-signer while your case is active.

Confirmation

Court approval of a Chapter 13 repayment plan. The judge reviews whether the plan meets all legal requirements and is feasible.

Cram Down

Reducing a secured debt to the current value of the collateral. In Chapter 13, vehicle loans older than 910 days can be crammed down to the car's current value.

Credit Counseling

A required course you must complete within 180 days before filing. A second course (debtor education) is required after filing but before discharge. Both are available online for about $20-50 each.

Creditor Matrix

A list of all your creditors with their names and mailing addresses. This is required at filing so the court can notify creditors.

Discharge

The court order that eliminates your personal liability for qualifying debts. After discharge, creditors cannot collect on those debts. The discharge injunction (section 524) permanently bars collection.

Dismissal

Termination of a bankruptcy case without a discharge. You still owe all debts. Can be voluntary or involuntary. A dismissed case may affect future filings.

Domestic Support Obligation (DSO)

Child support, alimony, and other support obligations. These cannot be discharged and collection continues during bankruptcy.

E-M

Exemptions

State and federal laws that protect certain property from the bankruptcy estate. New York lets you choose between state exemptions (homestead $179,975--$215,550) and federal exemptions (homestead $27,900). See New York Exemptions.

Fraudulent Transfer

Transferring property for less than fair value to keep it from creditors. The trustee can reverse transfers made within 2 years before filing (or longer under state law).

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. New York: $179,975--$215,550 by county. Federal: $27,900 (individual).

Lien

A legal claim on property as security for a debt. Liens survive bankruptcy - the discharge eliminates your personal obligation, but the lien remains on the property.

Lien Stripping

Removing a junior lien (like a second mortgage) from property when the property's value does not support it. Available in Chapter 13 in New York.

Means Test

The income-based formula that determines Chapter 7 eligibility. Compares your income to the state median. New York 1-person median: $66,973. See Means Test in New York.

Meeting of Creditors (341 Meeting)

Required hearing where the trustee questions you under oath about your finances. Typically 5-10 minutes, about 30 days after filing. See 341 Meeting Guide.

N-Z

No-Asset Case

A Chapter 7 case where the debtor has no non-exempt property for the trustee to liquidate. The vast majority of Chapter 7 cases are no-asset.

Nondischargeable Debt

Debts that survive bankruptcy. Examples: student loans (unless undue hardship is proven), most tax debts, child support, alimony, debts from fraud, DUI judgments, and certain fines. See Debt Types.

Petition

The official document you file to begin a bankruptcy case (Official Form 101). Filing the petition creates the bankruptcy estate and triggers the automatic stay.

Plan (Chapter 13)

The document proposing how you will repay creditors over 3-5 years. Must pay priority debts in full, pay secured creditors at least the value of collateral, and pay unsecured creditors at least what they would receive in Chapter 7.

Preference Payment

Payment of more than $600 to a creditor within 90 days before filing (or 1 year if the creditor is an insider). The trustee can recover these payments for equal distribution.

Priority Debt

Debts that must be paid first in bankruptcy, including domestic support obligations, recent tax debts, and administrative expenses.

Proof of Claim

A form a creditor files to assert that you owe them money. The trustee reviews these claims for accuracy.

Reaffirmation Agreement

A voluntary agreement to continue paying a dischargeable debt, usually to keep collateral like a vehicle. Must be approved by the court if you are not represented by an attorney.

Redemption

Paying the current value of collateral in a lump sum to keep it, even if you owe more than it is worth. Available in Chapter 7 for personal property used for personal purposes.

Schedules

The detailed forms listing all your assets, debts, income, expenses, and financial transactions. Required within 14 days of filing.

Secured Debt

Debt backed by collateral (mortgage, car loan). The lien survives bankruptcy. You must pay to keep the property.

Trustee

The person appointed to administer your bankruptcy case. In Chapter 7, the trustee looks for non-exempt assets to sell. In Chapter 13, the trustee collects and distributes your plan payments.

Unsecured Debt

Debt with no collateral backing it. Credit cards, medical bills, personal loans. Generally dischargeable in bankruptcy.

U.S. Trustee

A Department of Justice official who oversees the bankruptcy system. Different from the case trustee. Reviews means tests and monitors for abuse.

Need More Help?

Free Discharge Screener
Full FAQ

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home1328(f) ScreenerCh. 7 vs 13Automatic Stay341 MeetingMeans TestCost GuideDismissed?File Again?How to File

## Bankruptcy Income Limits in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/income-limits.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Bankruptcy Income Limits in New York

Bankruptcy Income Limits in New York

Income determines which chapter you qualify for and how much you pay. Here are the current thresholds for New York residents filing in the S.D.N.Y.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

New York Median Income (Means Test)

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthlyChapter 7Chapter 13 Plan Length

1$66,973$5,581Auto-qualify if below3 years if below

2$82,282$6,857Auto-qualify if below3 years if below

3$97,878$8,157Auto-qualify if below3 years if below

4$119,164$9,930Auto-qualify if below3 years if below

Add $10,300 for each additional household member. Figures published by the U.S. Trustee Program, updated periodically.

What Counts as Income

The means test uses your average monthly income from the 6 full calendar months before filing. It includes:

Wages, salary, tips, overtime, bonuses, commissions

Net self-employment or business income

Rental and investment income

Pension and retirement distributions

Unemployment compensation and workers' compensation

Child support and alimony received

Regular contributions from a non-filing spouse or household member

What Does NOT Count

Social Security benefits (for the means test comparison, though they count for disposable income in Chapter 13)

Payments to victims of war crimes or terrorism

Timing strategy: Because the test uses a 6-month lookback, filing during a period of lower income (after job loss, end of seasonal work, after a bonus period ends) can help you qualify for Chapter 7.

Above Median? You May Still Qualify

Being above the median does not automatically disqualify you from Chapter 7. The second part of the means test subtracts allowed expenses from your income. New York's high cost of living means many above-median filers still pass after deducting:

Actual mortgage or rent payments

Property taxes and insurance

Health insurance and medical costs

Childcare and education

Payroll taxes

Court-ordered payments

Vehicle loan and lease payments

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7?

New York's median for 1 person is $66,973. Below = auto-qualify. Above-median filers may still qualify after expense deductions. See Chapter 7 eligibility for details.

Is there an income limit for Chapter 13?

No ceiling. Income determines your plan length (3 or 5 years) and payment amount, not eligibility. The debt limit is $2,750,000. See Chapter 13 eligibility.

Can I make too much to file any bankruptcy?

No. If you earn too much for Chapter 7, file Chapter 13. If debts exceed Chapter 13 limits, file Chapter 11. There is always an option.

Does my spouse's income count?

If you are married and not filing jointly, your spouse's income is included on the means test form but is then subtracted if the spouse is not paying for household expenses. A non-filing spouse's income that contributes to household expenses is counted.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## New York Bankruptcy - Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/

Skip to main content

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP Rule 9037 PII Scanner

New York Bankruptcy - Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Over 25,112 New York area residents have filed for bankruptcy since 2008. This free guide will help you understand your options, know what to expect, and make informed decisions.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

25,112

S.D.N.Y. Cases (2008-2024)

59.0%

Dismissal Rate

41.0%

Discharge Rate

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Southern District of New York at a Glance

Understanding local court data can help you make more informed decisions about filing bankruptcy in New York.

S.D.N.Y. Dismissal Rate

59.0% dismissed

Out of 25,112 Chapter 13 cases filed between 2008 and 2024, 59.0% were dismissed before plan completion. Only 41.0% achieved a successful discharge.

Courthouse: 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

Key Metrics

24.9%

Prior filer rate - 24.9% of filers had a previous bankruptcy case. Pro se rate: 18.9%. Estimated 1328(f) violations: 457.

View full statistics →

What to Expect When Filing

The Process at a Glance

Credit counseling - You must complete a credit counseling course within 180 days before filing. Cost: about $15-25.

File your petition - Your attorney files the petition, schedules, and statements with the court. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, you attend a brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education course - You must complete a second financial education course before discharge. Cost: about $10-25.

Discharge - Chapter 7: about 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Detailed timeline and what to expect →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost?

The total cost depends on the chapter you file and your specific situation:

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,500 - $3,500 for Chapter 7; $3,500 - $6,000 for Chapter 13 in New York

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in New York?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in New York typically range from $1,500 - $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,500 - $6,000 for Chapter 13. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in New York?

New York's homestead exemption protects up to $179,975 - $215,550 (varies by county) of equity in your home. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments. See all exemptions.

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy?

New York allows a vehicle exemption of $4,825. If your car equity is below that amount, you can keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments. Learn about exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors usually happens about 30 days after filing. Full timeline.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt quickly but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 allows you to keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Compare chapters.

See all frequently asked questions →

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Judgments and Bankruptcy in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/judgments.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Judgments and Bankruptcy in New York

Judgments and Bankruptcy in New York

A creditor judgment can lead to wage garnishment, bank levies, and property liens. Bankruptcy can discharge the debt and, in many cases, remove the lien.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Discharging the Judgment Debt

Whether a judgment is dischargeable depends on the underlying debt, not the judgment itself:

Dischargeable: Judgments from credit card lawsuits, medical debt, personal loans, breach of contract, and most other civil claims

NOT dischargeable: Judgments from fraud (section 523(a)(2)), DUI injury (section 523(a)(6)), willful and malicious injury, student loans, and domestic support obligations

After discharge, the creditor cannot collect on the judgment debt. The discharge injunction permanently bars collection.

Judgment Liens in New York

In New York, when a creditor dockets a money judgment, it automatically creates a lien on all real property you own in that county. This lien lasts 10 years and can be renewed.

The discharge eliminates your personal liability, but the lien itself can survive unless you take action:

Lien Avoidance (Section 522(f))

You can file a motion to avoid a judicial lien if it impairs your exemptions. In New York, this means the lien can be stripped if:

The lien is on your homestead, AND

Your equity minus the lien is less than your homestead exemption ($179,975--$215,550 by county)

This motion must be filed during the bankruptcy case. If you do not file it, the lien survives the discharge.

Stopping Garnishment and Bank Levies

New York judgment creditors can:

Garnish wages - the lesser of 10% of gross or 25% of disposable earnings

Freeze bank accounts with a restraining notice (accounts under $3,600 are exempt)

Seize property through the county sheriff

The automatic stay stops all of these immediately upon filing. Your employer must stop withholding once notified of the bankruptcy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge a money judgment in bankruptcy?

Generally yes, if the underlying debt is dischargeable. Credit card, medical, and contract judgments can be discharged. Fraud, DUI, and child support judgments cannot.

What about judgment liens on my property?

Judgment liens can survive discharge. File a motion under section 522(f) to avoid the lien if it impairs your exemptions. In New York, the homestead exemption is $179,975--$215,550.

Does bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes, immediately. The automatic stay stops all garnishment. In New York, creditors can garnish 10% of gross or 25% of disposable wages (whichever is less). Filing stops it the same day.

Can I get money back that was already garnished?

Possibly. Garnishments within 90 days before filing that total over $600 may be recoverable as preferential transfers. The trustee can pursue these recoveries for the benefit of the estate.

Free Discharge Screener
Automatic Stay Guide

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New York - $4,825 Vehicle Exemption
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/keep-your-car.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Keep Your Car

Can You Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in New York?

In most cases, yes. New York's vehicle exemption and bankruptcy protections let the majority of filers keep their vehicles. Here is how it works in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

New York Vehicle Exemption: $4,825

New York's vehicle exemption under CPLR 5205(h) protects up to $4,825 of equity in one motor vehicle. Equity is your car's fair market value minus the loan balance.

Example: If your car is worth $12,000 and you owe $9,000, your equity is $3,000 - well within the $4,825 exemption. You keep the car.

If your equity exceeds $4,825: In Chapter 7, the trustee could potentially sell the vehicle and return the exempt amount to you. However, trustees rarely sell cars with marginal excess equity because of the costs involved.

State vs. Federal Exemptions

New York allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions. You cannot mix and match.

SystemVehicle ExemptionWildcard Available?

New York State$4,825$1,150 general wildcard

Federal (522(d))$4,450Up to $14,875 (unused homestead wildcard)

If you are a renter (no homestead to protect), the federal exemptions may be better because the unused portion of the homestead exemption can be applied as a wildcard to your vehicle or other property.

Keeping Your Car in Chapter 7

In Chapter 7, you have three options for a financed vehicle:

Reaffirmation - Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The debt survives your discharge. You keep the car and the payment. Courts require you to show you can afford it.

Redemption - Pay the lender the car's current fair market value in one lump sum. Useful when you owe more than the car is worth, but you need the cash to do it.

Surrender - Give the car back. The remaining loan balance is discharged. Good option if the car is worth less than what you owe and the payments are unaffordable.

Keeping Your Car in Chapter 13

Chapter 13 is often the better choice for vehicle protection because:

Catch up on missed payments - Past-due amounts are folded into your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Cramdown option - If you purchased the car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current value. The rest becomes unsecured debt.

Lower interest rate - The court may reduce your interest rate to the prime rate plus a risk adjustment (typically 5-7% total).

No repossession - The automatic stay protects your car throughout the plan.

Cramdown example: You owe $18,000 on a car worth $11,000 (purchased 3 years ago). In Chapter 13, you pay only $11,000 as a secured claim. The remaining $7,000 is treated as unsecured debt and may be partially or fully discharged.

What If Your Car Was Just Repossessed?

If your car was recently repossessed but not yet sold, filing bankruptcy may get it back. The automatic stay requires the lender to return the vehicle if it has not been sold at auction. Time is critical - act quickly.

In New York, creditors must provide notice before selling a repossessed vehicle. If you file bankruptcy before the sale, the stay applies and you may be able to recover the car through a Chapter 13 plan. See repossession and bankruptcy for more details.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in New York?

In most cases, yes. New York's vehicle exemption protects up to $4,825 of equity. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on missed payments through your plan. Learn more.

What is the New York vehicle exemption?

New York protects $4,825 of equity in one motor vehicle under state exemptions. If you choose federal exemptions, the vehicle exemption is $4,450 plus you may apply unused homestead wildcard. Full exemption details.

What is reaffirmation in Chapter 7?

Reaffirmation is an agreement to continue paying a car loan after Chapter 7. You keep the car and the loan survives your discharge. Courts review these to make sure you can afford the payments.

What is a cramdown in Chapter 13?

If you purchased your car more than 910 days before filing, Chapter 13 lets you pay only the car's current value rather than the full loan balance. The rest becomes unsecured debt.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Repossession
Keep Your House
Automatic Stay
Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Debt Types

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in New York - Homestead Exemption $179,975-$215,550
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/keep-your-house.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Keep Your House

Can You Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in New York?

New York's homestead exemption protects $179,975 to $215,550 of equity in your home, depending on the county. Most homeowners keep their homes in bankruptcy - especially in Chapter 13.

New York Homestead Exemption by County

Under CPLR 5206, New York's homestead exemption varies by county. The exemption protects equity in your primary residence - the difference between your home's fair market value and the total of all mortgages and liens.

County/RegionHomestead Exemption

Kings (Brooklyn), New York (Manhattan), Queens, Bronx, Richmond (Staten Island)$179,975

Westchester, Rockland, Putnam, Nassau, Suffolk$215,550

Dutchess, Orange, Ulster, Sullivan, Columbia$179,975

Example: Your Manhattan co-op is worth $500,000. You owe $380,000 on the mortgage. Your equity is $120,000 - well within the $179,975 exemption. You keep your home.

Keeping Your Home in Chapter 7

In Chapter 7, you keep your home if your equity is within the exemption limit. Key points:

Equity within exemption: You keep the home. The trustee has no interest in selling it.

Must stay current on mortgage: The automatic stay protects you during the case, but you must continue making payments to keep the home.

Reaffirmation not always required: In New York, many lenders do not require reaffirmation of a mortgage. As long as you keep paying, you keep the home.

Excess equity: If your equity exceeds the exemption, the trustee could sell the home, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to creditors. This is rare in practice.

Keeping Your Home in Chapter 13

Chapter 13 is the most powerful tool for saving a home from foreclosure:

Stop foreclosure immediately - The automatic stay halts foreclosure proceedings the moment you file.

Catch up on arrears - Missed mortgage payments are spread over your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Strip second mortgages - If your home is worth less than your first mortgage balance, a second mortgage or HELOC can be reclassified as unsecured debt and potentially discharged.

Protection for the full plan - The automatic stay lasts throughout the entire 3-5 year plan as long as you make payments.

Important: You must continue making regular monthly mortgage payments going forward in addition to the plan payments that cure the arrears.

State vs. Federal Exemptions for Homeowners

New York State Exemptions

Homestead: $179,975-$215,550

Better for homeowners with significant equity

Vehicle: $4,825

Wildcard: $1,150

Federal Exemptions

Homestead: $27,900

Better for renters or minimal home equity

Vehicle: $4,450

Wildcard: up to $14,875 (unused homestead)

If you own a home, state exemptions almost always provide better protection. If you rent, consider federal exemptions for the larger wildcard. Full exemption comparison.

Co-ops and Condos

New York's homestead exemption applies to co-ops and condos, not just houses. The exemption protects your ownership interest (shares in a co-op, deed interest in a condo) up to the applicable county limit.

For co-op owners, the equity calculation considers the fair market value of your shares minus any underlying mortgage and share loan balance.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my house if I file bankruptcy in New York?

In most cases, yes. New York's homestead exemption protects $179,975-$215,550 of equity depending on the county. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments. Learn more.

What is the New York homestead exemption?

It protects equity in your primary residence: $179,975 in the five NYC boroughs and most upstate counties, and $215,550 in higher-cost suburban counties like Westchester, Nassau, and Suffolk. Full exemption details.

Can Chapter 13 stop foreclosure?

Yes. Filing Chapter 13 immediately stops foreclosure through the automatic stay. You can catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year plan while continuing regular monthly payments.

Should I choose state or federal exemptions?

Homeowners should almost always choose state exemptions for the higher homestead protection. Renters or those with minimal home equity may benefit from federal exemptions with the larger wildcard.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Free Legal Help for Bankruptcy in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/legal-aid.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Free Legal Help for Bankruptcy in New York | New York Bankruptcy Guide›Free Legal Help for Bankruptcy in New York City

Free Legal Help for Bankruptcy in New York City

If you are struggling with debt but cannot afford a bankruptcy attorney, these New York City organizations may be able to help - for free or at reduced cost.

New York City Legal Aid Organizations

The Legal Aid Society

Phone: (212) 577-3300

Website: legal-aid.org

The Legal Aid Society is the nation's oldest and largest legal aid organization. They provide free civil legal assistance to low-income New Yorkers across all five boroughs, handling a wide range of matters including consumer debt, foreclosure defense, and bankruptcy-related cases.

New York Legal Assistance Group (NYLAG)

Phone: (212) 613-5000

NYLAG provides free civil legal services to low-income New Yorkers. Their consumer protection and bankruptcy practice helps individuals navigate debt relief options, defend against creditor lawsuits, and understand their rights under federal and state law.

Legal Services NYC

Phone: (917) 661-4500

Legal Services NYC is the largest civil legal services provider in the country. They operate offices in all five boroughs and provide free legal assistance to low-income residents on matters including consumer debt, public benefits, housing, and bankruptcy.

Bronx Legal Services

Bronx Legal Services provides free legal assistance to low-income residents of the Bronx. As part of the Legal Services NYC network, they offer help with consumer debt issues, creditor harassment, and other civil legal matters. Contact Legal Services NYC for Bronx-specific intake information.

Who Qualifies for Legal Aid?

Most legal aid organizations serve people whose household income is at or below 125-200% of the federal poverty level. For reference, the 2026 guidelines are approximately:

Single person: $15,060 (100% FPL) - up to ~$30,120 (200% FPL)

Family of 2: $20,440 - up to ~$40,880

Family of 4: $31,200 - up to ~$62,400

Each organization sets its own income limits. Even if you are slightly above the threshold, it is worth calling - some programs make exceptions based on circumstances such as medical emergencies, domestic violence, or sudden job loss.

Filing on Your Own (Pro Se)

If you do not qualify for legal aid but cannot afford an attorney, you have the right to file bankruptcy on your own. This is called filing "pro se." It is more common in Chapter 7 cases, which are generally simpler than Chapter 13.

The Pro Se Bankruptcy Guide provides step-by-step instructions for people filing without an attorney.

You can also reduce costs by:

Requesting a filing fee waiver (Chapter 7 only, if income is below 150% FPL)

Paying the filing fee in installments

Using free credit counseling agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee

Pro Se Filing Guide

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I get free bankruptcy help in New York City?

Yes. Several organizations in New York City provide free legal assistance for bankruptcy cases, including the Legal Aid Society, NYLAG, and Legal Services NYC. Eligibility is generally based on income, typically at or below 125-200% of the federal poverty level.

Do I qualify for legal aid?

Most legal aid organizations in New York serve people whose income is at or below 125-200% of the federal poverty level. For a single person in 2026, that is roughly $18,000-$30,000 per year. Family size affects the threshold. Contact the organization directly for specific eligibility requirements.

What if I make too much for legal aid?

If your income is too high for legal aid, consider filing pro se (on your own) using resources at prosebankruptcy.org. Some attorneys offer payment plans or flat-fee arrangements. NYLAG and other organizations may also offer brief advice consultations regardless of income.

What documents do I need to apply for free bankruptcy help?

Most legal aid organizations will ask for proof of income (pay stubs, tax returns, benefits letters), identification, and a summary of your debts and assets. Bring as much documentation as you can to your first appointment to speed up the process.

## New York City Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/means-test.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›New York Bankruptcy Guide›Means Test

New York City Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?

The means test determines whether you can file Chapter 7 bankruptcy. Your income is compared to the median income in New York for your household size. New York has among the highest median incomes in the nation, which means more residents qualify.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is the Means Test?

The means test was introduced by the Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act of 2005 (BAPCPA). Its purpose is to determine whether a debtor's Chapter 7 filing constitutes an "abuse" of the bankruptcy system based on income.

The test works in two parts:

Part 1 - Income comparison: Your average monthly income over the past six months is compared to the median income for your household size in New York. If you are below median, you qualify for Chapter 7 automatically.

Part 2 - Disposable income calculation: If you are above median, the test subtracts allowed expenses from your income. If your remaining disposable income is too low to fund a meaningful Chapter 13 plan, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

2026 New York Median Income Thresholds

New York's high cost of living translates to higher median income figures, which means the means test threshold is higher than in most states. More New Yorkers pass Part 1 automatically.

Household SizeAnnual Median IncomeMonthly Equivalent

1 person$66,973$5,581

2 persons$85,474$7,123

3 persons$103,286$8,607

4 persons$124,598$10,383

Each additional personAdd approximately $9,900

New York City filers are in the Southern District of New York. Courthouse: One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004.

New York Exemption Choice: State vs. Federal

New York is one of the states that allows debtors to choose between the state exemption system and the federal exemption system under 11 U.S.C. section 522(d). You cannot mix and match - you must use one system entirely.

This choice can significantly affect the means test calculation in Part 2, because allowed deductions differ between the two systems. An experienced bankruptcy attorney will calculate both options to determine which protects more of your property and produces a better means test result.

New York State Exemptions

Homestead: $179,975-$215,550 (varies by county)

Vehicle: $4,825 per person

Wildcard: limited

Better for homeowners with significant equity

Federal Exemptions

Homestead: $27,900 (individual)

Vehicle: $4,450 per person

Wildcard: $1,475 + up to $13,950 of unused homestead

Better for renters or those with low home equity

Step-by-Step Means Test Walkthrough

1Calculate your current monthly income (CMI)

Add up all income from all sources for the six full calendar months before your filing date. Divide by six to get your average monthly income. Multiply by 12 for your annualized figure. Sources include wages, salary, tips, bonuses, overtime, self-employment income, rental income, pension, disability (non-Social Security), alimony received, and any other regular income.

2Determine your household size

Count everyone living in your household who depends on your income. This typically includes yourself, your spouse (even if not filing jointly), and dependent children. In multi-generational households common in New York City, this determination can be especially important.

3Compare to median income

Compare your annualized CMI to the New York median income for your household size (see table above). If your income is at or below the median, you pass the means test and qualify for Chapter 7. No further calculation is needed.

4If above median: Calculate allowable deductions

If your income exceeds the median, you proceed to Part 2. You deduct IRS-approved living expenses, actual secured debt payments (mortgage, car loans), priority debts, and certain other allowed expenses. New York City's high housing costs and commuting expenses can significantly reduce disposable income in this calculation.

5Calculate disposable income

Subtract your total allowed deductions from your CMI. Multiply by 60 (representing a 5-year Chapter 13 plan). If the result is less than $9,075, you pass. If more than $15,150, you fail. Between those amounts, further analysis is needed.

6File the appropriate form

The means test is filed on Official Form 122A-1 (Chapter 7 Statement of Your Current Monthly Income) and, if needed, Form 122A-2 (Chapter 7 Means Test Calculation). Your attorney will prepare these forms as part of your petition.

What Happens If You Fail the Means Test?

Failing the means test does not mean you cannot file bankruptcy. You have several options:

File Chapter 13 instead: Chapter 13 has no means test. You repay debts over 3-5 years while keeping your property.

Challenge with special circumstances: Rebut the presumption of abuse by documenting serious medical conditions, military service, or factors that justify additional expenses. New York's high cost of living can support these arguments.

Wait and refile: Since the means test uses the six months before filing, you may qualify later if your income decreases.

Re-examine deductions: An experienced attorney may find overlooked deductions - health insurance, mandatory retirement contributions, childcare, education expenses.

File Chapter 11 Subchapter V: For small business owners, Subchapter V may be an option.

Multi-Borough Considerations

New York City bankruptcy cases are filed in the Southern District of New York. All five boroughs fall within the S.D.N.Y., though cases may be assigned to different courthouses:

Manhattan and the Bronx: One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

Westchester County and surrounding: 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

Regardless of which borough you live in, the same New York state median income figures and exemptions apply. The S.D.N.Y. has its own local rules that may affect filing procedures, trustee assignments, and hearing schedules.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the bankruptcy means test?

The means test compares your income to the median income in New York for your household size. If below, you qualify for Chapter 7 automatically. If above, a second calculation factors in allowed expenses. Learn more about the means test.

What is the median income for the means test in New York in 2026?

The 2026 New York thresholds are: 1-person $66,973, 2-person $85,474, 3-person $103,286, 4-person $124,598. Add approximately $9,900 for each additional person.

Can I choose federal or state exemptions in New York?

Yes. New York allows debtors to choose between state and federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. section 522(d). You cannot mix and match. An attorney can determine which system protects more of your property.

What happens if I fail the means test?

You can file Chapter 13 instead (no means test), wait until your income drops, challenge with special circumstances, or re-examine deductions. Failing does not mean you cannot file bankruptcy.

Does the means test apply to Chapter 13?

No. The means test does not apply to Chapter 13. However, your income level determines whether your Chapter 13 plan lasts 3 years (below median) or 5 years (above median).

What income counts for the means test?

All income from all sources over the six months before filing, including wages, salary, tips, bonuses, overtime, business income, rental income, pension, and unemployment. Social Security benefits are excluded from the initial median income comparison.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Can I File Bankruptcy on Medical Debt Only?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/medical-debt-only.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Can I File Bankruptcy on Medical Debt Only?

Can I File Bankruptcy on Medical Debt Only?

A free guide for New York residents. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

You cannot selectively file bankruptcy on just one type of debt - all debts must be listed in your petition. However, medical debt IS fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. If medical bills are your primary burden, Chapter 7 may eliminate them entirely in 3-4 months. Before filing, check if your hospital has charity care programs, negotiate directly, or apply for financial assistance. Many hospitals write off bills for patients below 200-400% of the federal poverty level.

In New York:

Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I File Bankruptcy on Medical Debt Only?

You cannot selectively file bankruptcy on just one type of debt - all debts must be listed in your petition. However, medical debt IS fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. If medical bills are your primary burden, Chapter 7 may eliminate them entirely in 3-4 months. Before filing, check if your hospital has charity care programs, negotiate directly, or apply for financial assistance. Many hospitals write off bills for patients below 200-400% of the federal poverty level.

How does this work in New York?

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

Where do I file?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in New York - Eliminate Hospital Bills
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/medical-debt.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Medical Debt

Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in New York

Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy filings in America. Both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 can eliminate medical bills entirely - no matter how large the balance.

Medical Debt Is Fully Dischargeable

Medical debt is classified as general unsecured debt under bankruptcy law. This means it is 100% dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13:

Hospital bills (ER visits, surgeries, inpatient stays)

Doctor and specialist bills

Dental and vision debt

Ambulance and transport bills

Lab work and imaging charges

Medical debt in collections

Medical debt judgments

Credit card debt used to pay medical bills

There is no minimum or maximum amount. Whether you owe $2,000 or $200,000, bankruptcy can eliminate it.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Medical Debt

Chapter 7 - Fastest Relief

If medical debt is your primary problem and you pass the means test (income below $66,973 for a single filer in New York), Chapter 7 eliminates medical bills in 3-4 months. You keep exempt property and walk away debt-free.

Best for: People whose primary debt is medical, with income below the median, and no assets at risk.

Chapter 13 - Comprehensive Solution

If medical debt is part of a larger problem (behind on mortgage, car loan, taxes), Chapter 13 lets you address everything in one plan. Medical debt is typically paid pennies on the dollar or nothing through the plan.

Best for: People who need to catch up on a mortgage or car payment while also eliminating medical debt.

Before Bankruptcy: Hospital Financial Assistance

New York law requires nonprofit hospitals to offer financial assistance programs. Before filing bankruptcy, consider requesting a reduction or write-off from your hospital:

NYC Health+Hospitals - The nation's largest public hospital system. Offers sliding-scale fees and financial assistance for uninsured and underinsured patients.

NYU Langone Health - Financial assistance program for qualifying patients. Generally covers those at or below 300% of the federal poverty level.

Mount Sinai Health System - Charity care and financial assistance available. Applications accepted at all Mount Sinai locations.

NewYork-Presbyterian - Financial assistance for emergency and medically necessary care.

Under New York's Surprise Bill Law, you are also protected from unexpected out-of-network charges for emergency services.

Medical Debt and Credit Reports

Recent changes to credit reporting have reduced the impact of medical debt:

Paid medical collections are removed from credit reports

Unpaid medical debt under $500 is no longer reported

Medical debt has a 1-year waiting period before it can appear on credit reports

However, large unpaid medical debts and medical debt judgments still impact your credit significantly. Bankruptcy provides a clean break and a path to rebuild your credit.

What About Medical Liens?

If a medical provider or collection agency has obtained a court judgment and placed a lien on your property, the situation is more complex. The lien may need to be addressed separately in bankruptcy:

Chapter 7: You can file a motion to avoid a judicial lien on exempt property under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f).

Chapter 13: Judgment liens can be addressed through the plan.

New York's generous exemptions help protect your property from medical debt liens.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy eliminate medical debt?

Yes. Medical debt is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. There is no minimum or maximum amount.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 for medical debt?

If medical debt is your primary problem and you pass the means test, Chapter 7 is usually faster - bills eliminated in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 is better if you also need to catch up on a mortgage or car loan.

Will bankruptcy stop medical debt collection calls?

Yes. The automatic stay takes effect the instant you file, stopping all collection activity including calls, letters, lawsuits, and wage garnishments.

Should I negotiate before filing bankruptcy?

If medical debt is your only problem, contact the hospital's financial assistance program first. Many NYC hospitals (NYC Health+Hospitals, NYU Langone, Mount Sinai) offer significant reductions. If the total debt is unmanageable, bankruptcy provides complete relief.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/payday-loans.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in New York

Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in New York

New York bans payday lending, but many residents still carry online payday loan debt. Bankruptcy can discharge these loans, with some important caveats.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

New York's Payday Lending Ban

New York prohibits payday lending through its usury laws, which cap interest rates at 16% for most lenders and 25% for licensed lenders. Traditional payday loans (with APRs often exceeding 400%) are illegal in New York.

However, many New Yorkers obtain high-interest loans from:

Online lenders based in other states or offshore

Tribal lenders that claim sovereign immunity from state lending laws

Installment lenders that structure loans to technically comply while charging very high rates

Regardless of the lender's legal status, these debts can generally be discharged in bankruptcy.

Discharging Payday Loan Debt

Payday loans are unsecured debt. In Chapter 7, they are eliminated in 3-4 months. In Chapter 13, you pay a portion over 3-5 years.

Potential Challenges to Discharge

Fraud claim. If you provided false information on the loan application (fake employer, inflated income), the lender could argue the debt is nondischargeable under section 523(a)(2).

No-intent-to-repay. Taking a payday loan knowing you will file bankruptcy soon could be challenged, but the lender bears the burden of proof.

70-day presumption. Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days of filing are presumed nondischargeable, and payday loans may be treated as cash advances.

In practice, payday lenders rarely challenge discharge because the amounts are small relative to litigation costs.

Stopping Collection

The automatic stay stops all payday loan collection the moment you file:

ACH debits - revoke authorization with your bank and the lender immediately upon filing. Any post-filing debits are stay violations.

Collection calls - all calls must stop.

Threats of criminal prosecution - it is not a crime to default on a payday loan. Threats of arrest are illegal under the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act.

Post-dated checks - the lender cannot deposit post-dated checks after filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can payday loans be discharged in bankruptcy?

Yes. Payday loans are unsecured debt and are generally dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. The lender would have to prove fraud to prevent discharge, which is rare for small-dollar payday loans.

Are payday loans legal in New York?

No. New York's usury laws effectively ban payday lending. However, online and tribal lenders still make loans to New York residents, creating debts that can be addressed through bankruptcy.

Can a payday lender debit my account after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection including ACH debits. Revoke authorization immediately upon filing and notify your bank. Post-filing debits are stay violations.

Can I go to jail for not paying a payday loan?

No. Failure to repay a payday loan is a civil matter, not a criminal one. Threats of arrest or criminal prosecution for unpaid payday loans are illegal under the FDCPA.

Check Your Discharge Eligibility

Free Discharge Screener
Debt Types Guide

## Can I Undo a Reaffirmation Agreement?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/reaffirmation-regret.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Can I Undo a Reaffirmation Agreement?

Can I Undo a Reaffirmation Agreement?

A free guide for New York residents. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

You have 60 days after the reaffirmation agreement is filed with the court to rescind (cancel) it. After 60 days, your options are limited. You can voluntarily surrender the property, refinance the loan, or negotiate with the creditor. If the reaffirmation was not properly disclosed or you were not represented by counsel when signing, there may be grounds to challenge it. Common with car loans where payments become unaffordable post-bankruptcy.

In New York:

Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I Undo a Reaffirmation Agreement?

You have 60 days after the reaffirmation agreement is filed with the court to rescind (cancel) it. After 60 days, your options are limited. You can voluntarily surrender the property, refinance the loan, or negotiate with the creditor. If the reaffirmation was not properly disclosed or you were not represented by counsel when signing, there may be grounds to challenge it. Common with car loans where payments become unaffordable post-bankruptcy.

How does this work in New York?

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

Where do I file?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Renting an Apartment After Bankruptcy
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/renting-after-bankruptcy.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Renting an Apartment After Bankruptcy

Renting an Apartment After Bankruptcy

A free guide for New York residents. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

Finding an apartment after bankruptcy is possible but requires strategy. Most large property management companies run credit checks and will see the bankruptcy. Smaller landlords and individual owners are often more flexible. Tips: offer a larger security deposit, provide references from previous landlords, bring proof of income, explain the bankruptcy briefly and honestly, consider a co-signer, and look for landlords who advertise 'no credit check' or 'second chance' rentals.

In New York: NYC has strong tenant protections. Rent-stabilized apartments generally cannot refuse renewal based on credit. Free-market rentals may screen.

Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Renting an Apartment After Bankruptcy

Finding an apartment after bankruptcy is possible but requires strategy. Most large property management companies run credit checks and will see the bankruptcy. Smaller landlords and individual owners are often more flexible. Tips: offer a larger security deposit, provide references from previous landlords, bring proof of income, explain the bankruptcy briefly and honestly, consider a co-signer, and look for landlords who advertise 'no credit check' or 'second chance' rentals.

How does this work in New York?

NYC has strong tenant protections. Rent-stabilized apartments generally cannot refuse renewal based on credit. Free-market rentals may screen. Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

Where do I file?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Can Bankruptcy Remove a Repossession From Your Credit?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/repo-credit-report.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Can Bankruptcy Remove a Repossession From Your Credit?

Can Bankruptcy Remove a Repossession From Your Credit?

A free guide for New York residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

Bankruptcy does not remove a repossession entry from your credit report. However, filing bankruptcy discharges the deficiency balance - the amount you still owe after the repo and sale. Your credit report should show the balance as $0 after discharge. If it still shows a balance, dispute it with the credit bureaus under the Fair Credit Reporting Act. The repo notation itself remains for 7 years.

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can Bankruptcy Remove a Repossession From Your Credit?

Bankruptcy does not remove a repossession entry from your credit report. However, filing bankruptcy discharges the deficiency balance - the amount you still owe after the repo and sale. Your credit report should show the balance as $0 after discharge. If it still shows a balance, dispute it with the credit bureaus under the Fair Credit Reporting Act. The repo notation itself remains for 7 years.

Does this apply in New York?

Yes. This applies to S.D.N.Y.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Repossession and Bankruptcy in New York - Stop Repo, Get Your Car Back
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/repossession.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Repossession

Repossession and Bankruptcy in New York

If your car is about to be repossessed - or already has been - bankruptcy may be able to stop the process and even get it back. Time is critical.

Before Repossession: Stop It with Bankruptcy

If you are behind on car payments and facing repossession, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately prohibits the lender from repossessing your vehicle. The lender must stop all collection activity.

Chapter 13: You can catch up on missed payments through a 3-5 year repayment plan while keeping the car.

Chapter 7: Provides temporary protection. You must reaffirm, redeem, or surrender the vehicle. Details on keeping your car.

After Repossession: Getting Your Car Back

If your car has already been repossessed but not yet sold at auction, filing bankruptcy (particularly Chapter 13) may force the lender to return it:

File immediately. The automatic stay takes effect the instant the petition is filed.

Notify the lender. Your attorney sends immediate notice that the stay is in effect and the vehicle must be returned.

Motion for turnover. If the lender refuses, the court can order turnover of the vehicle under 11 U.S.C. section 542.

Cure arrears through the plan. Missed payments are folded into your Chapter 13 plan.

Critical timing: Under New York's UCC Article 9, lenders must give you reasonable notice before selling a repossessed vehicle. You typically have 10-15 days. File bankruptcy before the sale date.

Chapter 13 Cramdown

If you purchased your car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing Chapter 13, you may be eligible for a cramdown:

The secured claim is reduced to the car's current fair market value

The difference between the loan balance and the value becomes unsecured debt

The interest rate may be reduced to prime plus a risk adjustment (typically 5-7%)

You pay the crammed-down amount over the 3-5 year plan

Example: You owe $20,000 on a car worth $12,000 (purchased 3 years ago). Cramdown reduces the secured claim to $12,000. The $8,000 difference is treated as unsecured debt and may be partially or fully discharged.

Deficiency Balances

If the car was already sold at auction for less than you owe, the remaining balance is a deficiency. In New York, lenders can pursue you for this amount. Bankruptcy handles deficiencies:

Chapter 7: The deficiency is general unsecured debt and is discharged.

Chapter 13: The deficiency is treated as unsecured debt in the plan, typically paid pennies on the dollar or nothing.

Your Rights Under New York Law

New York UCC Article 9 protects borrowers from unfair repossession practices:

No breach of peace: The repo agent cannot use force, threats, or enter a locked garage to take your car.

Reasonable notice: You must receive notice before the car is sold at auction.

Commercially reasonable sale: The lender must sell the car in a commercially reasonable manner.

Accounting: You are entitled to an accounting of the sale proceeds and any deficiency.

If the lender violated any of these rules, you may have defenses that reduce or eliminate the deficiency. Discuss with an attorney.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop repossession?

Yes. The automatic stay immediately stops repossession efforts. If the car was already repossessed but not sold, filing bankruptcy (especially Chapter 13) may force the lender to return it.

Can I get my car back after repossession?

If you file bankruptcy before the car is sold at auction, the automatic stay may require the lender to return it. Time is critical - act immediately after repossession. Learn more.

What is a cramdown on a car loan?

If you bought the car 910+ days before filing Chapter 13, you can reduce the secured claim to the car's current value. The remaining loan balance becomes unsecured debt, potentially saving thousands.

What happens to the deficiency after repo?

The deficiency (remaining balance after the car is sold) is unsecured debt that can be fully discharged in Chapter 7 or paid pennies on the dollar in Chapter 13.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Keep Your Car

## New York Bankruptcy Resources - Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/resources.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Resources

New York Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process.

Bankruptcy Court

Southern District of New York - Bankruptcy Court

Address: 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

Phone: (212) 668-2870

Website: www.nysb.uscourts.gov

PACER: ecf.nysb.uscourts.gov

Additional Courthouse Location

Address: One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

Phone: (212) 668-2870

Legal Aid Organizations

Legal Aid Society of New York

Provides free civil legal services to low-income individuals, including bankruptcy representation.

Phone: (212) 577-3300

Website: legal-aid.org

New York City Bar Association - Lawyer Referral Service

Can connect you with attorneys who handle bankruptcy cases, including some who offer reduced fees or payment plans.

Phone: (212) 626-7373

Website: nycbar.org

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required: a pre-filing credit counseling course and a post-filing debtor education course. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov - approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available for low-income filers)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

Time: About 60-90 minutes per course

U.S. Trustee Program

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases.

Region 2: (212) 510-0500

Website: justice.gov/ust

Where is the New York bankruptcy court?

Southern District of New York: 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601. Phone: (212) 668-2870.

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in New York?

Yes. Legal Aid Society of New York ((212) 577-3300) provides free bankruptcy representation for qualifying low-income individuals.

Where can I take the required credit counseling course?

The U.S. Trustee Program maintains a list at justice.gov/ust. Most offer courses online for $15-25.

## Can I Keep My 401k and Retirement in Bankruptcy?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/retirement-accounts.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Can I Keep My 401k and Retirement in Bankruptcy?

Can I Keep My 401k and Retirement in Bankruptcy?

A free guide for New York residents. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

Yes - ERISA-qualified retirement accounts (401k, 403b, pensions) are 100% exempt under federal law, regardless of value. This is one of the strongest protections in bankruptcy. Traditional and Roth IRAs are exempt up to $1,512,350 (adjusted periodically). SEP-IRAs and SIMPLE IRAs get the same ERISA protection as 401k plans. Your retirement savings are safe.

In New York: New York exempts all ERISA-qualified plans. State exemption for IRAs is also generous.

Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I Keep My 401k and Retirement in Bankruptcy?

Yes - ERISA-qualified retirement accounts (401k, 403b, pensions) are 100% exempt under federal law, regardless of value. This is one of the strongest protections in bankruptcy. Traditional and Roth IRAs are exempt up to $1,512,350 (adjusted periodically). SEP-IRAs and SIMPLE IRAs get the same ERISA protection as 401k plans. Your retirement savings are safe.

How does this work in New York?

New York exempts all ERISA-qualified plans. State exemption for IRAs is also generous. Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

Where do I file?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in New York
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/second-mortgage.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in New York

Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in New York

If your home is worth less than what you owe on your first mortgage, Chapter 13 can strip off the second mortgage entirely - turning it into dischargeable unsecured debt.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Lien Stripping in Chapter 13

Lien stripping removes a junior lien (second mortgage or HELOC) from your property when the home's value does not support it. Here is how it works:

Determine your home's value. Get an appraisal or use comparable sales data.

Compare to first mortgage balance. If the home is worth less than the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage has no secured value.

File a motion. Ask the court to reclassify the second mortgage as wholly unsecured.

Include in plan. The second mortgage is treated as unsecured debt in your Chapter 13 plan (often paid at 0-10 cents on the dollar).

Complete the plan. After finishing your 3-5 year plan, the lien is permanently removed from your property.

Chapter 7 vs Chapter 13 for Second Mortgages

Chapter 7

Cannot strip off second mortgages (Dewsnup v. Timm, 502 U.S. 410)

Can discharge personal liability on second mortgage

The lien remains on the property

If you surrender the home, both mortgages go away with it

Chapter 13

Can strip off second mortgages if fully unsecured

Must complete 3-5 year plan for the strip to take effect

Must stay current on first mortgage during the plan

Lien removed permanently upon plan completion

New York Considerations

High property values. In New York City and surrounding areas, many homes have appreciated significantly. Make sure your home is truly underwater before pursuing lien stripping.

Judicial foreclosure. New York's judicial foreclosure process (12-18 months) gives you time to file Chapter 13 and begin the lien stripping process.

Mandatory settlement conference. New York requires foreclosure settlement conferences, which can run in parallel with your bankruptcy case.

Homestead exemption. The $179,975--$215,550 homestead exemption protects equity from unsecured creditors but is separate from lien stripping analysis.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I strip off a second mortgage in bankruptcy?

Yes, in Chapter 13, if your home's value is less than the first mortgage balance. The second mortgage is reclassified as unsecured debt. This is not available in Chapter 7.

What about HELOCs?

HELOCs are treated the same as second mortgages. If fully unsecured, they can be stripped in Chapter 13.

What if my home has some equity above the first mortgage?

If your home is worth even $1 more than the first mortgage, the second mortgage is partially secured and cannot be stripped. The entire second mortgage must be wholly unsecured for lien stripping to work.

When is the lien actually removed?

The lien is removed upon successful completion of the Chapter 13 plan (3-5 years). If you default on the plan and the case is dismissed, the lien remains.

Free Discharge Screener
Keep Your House Guide

## Bankruptcy and Security Clearance
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/security-clearance.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Security Clearance

Bankruptcy and Security Clearance

A free guide for New York residents. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

Filing bankruptcy does NOT automatically revoke your security clearance. In fact, the Department of Defense adjudicative guidelines consider financial irresponsibility a greater risk than filing bankruptcy. Filing shows you are taking responsible steps to address your debt. Many clearance holders file bankruptcy without losing their clearance. The concern is unresolved debt, not the filing itself. Be upfront with your security officer.

In New York:

Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Bankruptcy and Security Clearance

Filing bankruptcy does NOT automatically revoke your security clearance. In fact, the Department of Defense adjudicative guidelines consider financial irresponsibility a greater risk than filing bankruptcy. Filing shows you are taking responsible steps to address your debt. Many clearance holders file bankruptcy without losing their clearance. The concern is unresolved debt, not the filing itself. Be upfront with your security officer.

How does this work in New York?

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

Where do I file?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

## Small Business Bankruptcy in New York - Chapter 7, 11, and Subchapter V
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/small-business.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Small Business

Small Business Bankruptcy in New York

New York small businesses have several bankruptcy options depending on whether the goal is to close the business or reorganize and continue operating. Subchapter V has made reorganization faster and more affordable.

Bankruptcy Options for Small Businesses

OptionPurposeDebt LimitFiling Fee

Chapter 7Liquidate and closeNo limit$338

Chapter 11ReorganizeNo limit$1,738

Subchapter VStreamlined reorganization$7.5 million$571

Chapter 13 (sole proprietors)Personal + business debtsNo limit (post-2024)$313

Subchapter V - Small Business Reorganization

Subchapter V of Chapter 11, created by the Small Business Reorganization Act of 2019, is specifically designed for small businesses:

Debt limit: Total noncontingent, liquidated debts under $7.5 million (at least 50% from business activity)

No creditors committee: Reduces cost and complexity

No disclosure statement: The plan goes directly to the court for confirmation

Debtor keeps ownership: No requirement for creditor consent to confirm the plan (cramdown available without the absolute priority rule)

Dedicated Sub V trustee: Facilitates the process (but does not take control of the business)

90-day plan deadline: The plan must be filed within 90 days of the petition date

3-5 year plan: Disposable income is paid to creditors over 3-5 years

Chapter 7 Business Liquidation

If the business cannot be saved, Chapter 7 liquidation provides an orderly wind-down:

A trustee takes control of business assets and sells them to pay creditors

The business ceases operations

For sole proprietors, personal liability for business debts can be discharged

LLCs and corporations do not receive a discharge - they simply cease to exist

Personal guarantees on business debts may need to be addressed in a separate personal filing

Sole Proprietors: Personal and Business Debts

If you operate as a sole proprietor (no LLC or corporate structure), your personal and business debts are intertwined. Options include:

Chapter 7: Eliminates personal liability for business debts. The business closes, but you get a fresh start.

Chapter 13: Keep operating your business while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for sole proprietors who want to continue the business.

Subchapter V: Available to individuals with business debts comprising at least 50% of total debt.

New York exemptions protect tools of trade up to $3,000, which can include business equipment for sole proprietors.

S.D.N.Y. Business Bankruptcy Courthouses

Manhattan: One Bowling Green, New York, NY 10004

White Plains: 300 Quarropas St, White Plains, NY 10601

The Southern District of New York handles some of the most complex business bankruptcies in the nation. For small business cases, Subchapter V has significantly reduced the cost and time required. Look up your district.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the bankruptcy options for small businesses in New York?

Chapter 7 (liquidation to close), Chapter 11 (reorganization), Subchapter V (streamlined reorganization for debts under $7.5M), and Chapter 13 for sole proprietors. The best option depends on whether you want to continue operating.

What is Subchapter V bankruptcy?

A streamlined Chapter 11 for businesses with under $7.5M in debt. No creditors committee, no disclosure statement, the debtor keeps ownership, and the plan must be filed within 90 days. It is faster and cheaper than traditional Chapter 11.

Can I keep my business open during bankruptcy?

Yes, in Chapter 11 and Subchapter V. The business operates as a debtor-in-possession while reorganizing. In Chapter 7, the business typically closes. The automatic stay protects the business during the case.

How much does small business bankruptcy cost?

Filing fees: Chapter 7 ($338), Chapter 11 ($1,738), Subchapter V ($571). Attorney fees for Chapter 11 in New York typically range from $15,000-$50,000+. Subchapter V cases generally cost less. Cost breakdown.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## New York Bankruptcy Statistics - 25,112 Cases Analyzed
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/statistics.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Statistics

New York Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from 25,112 Chapter 13 cases in the Southern District of New York, analyzed from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008-2024).

25,112

Total Ch. 13 Cases (2008-2024)

59.0%

S.D.N.Y. Dismissal Rate

41.0%

Discharge Rate

24.9%

Prior Filer Rate

457

Est. 1328(f) Violations

18.9%

Pro Se Rate

What These Numbers Mean for You

If you are considering Chapter 13 bankruptcy in New York, these statistics should inform your decision:

59.0% of Chapter 13 cases in S.D.N.Y. are dismissed. That means many filers spent months or years making payments and got no debt relief at the end.

24.9% of filers had a previous bankruptcy case. This "prior filer" rate suggests many people are cycling through the system without achieving lasting relief.

An estimated 457 cases may involve potential 1328(f) eligibility issues - filings where the debtor had a prior discharge within the statutory time bar.

Quality of representation matters enormously. The data shows that representation quality is one of the strongest predictors of plan completion.

District Overview

MetricS.D.N.Y.

Total Ch. 13 cases (2008-2024)25,112

Dismissal rate59.0%

Discharge rate41.0%

Prior filer rate24.9%

Pro se rate18.9%

Est. 1328(f) violations457

Key Takeaways

The Prior Filer Problem

24.9% of S.D.N.Y. filers had a prior bankruptcy case. This cycling through the system suggests the first filing failed to provide lasting relief - and raises questions about whether filers were properly screened under section 1328(f).

457 Potential 1328(f) Violations

An estimated 457 cases may involve potential eligibility violations under 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f) - filings where the debtor had a prior discharge within the statutory time bar and could never have received a new discharge. These cases consume court resources and cost consumers time and money for a legally impossible outcome.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many bankruptcy cases have been filed in New York?

Between 2008 and 2024, the Southern District of New York saw 25,112 Chapter 13 filings.

What is the Chapter 13 success rate in New York?

The discharge rate is 41.0% in S.D.N.Y. (2008-2024 data). The remaining 59.0% of cases were dismissed.

What does the prior filer rate mean?

24.9% of filers had a previous case. This suggests many are cycling through without lasting relief, and raises questions about 1328(f) eligibility screening.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## What Bills Can I Stop Paying Before Filing?
Source: https://newyorkbankruptcy.org/stop-paying-bills.html

New York Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›What Bills Can I Stop Paying Before Filing?

What Bills Can I Stop Paying Before Filing?

A free guide for New York residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D.N.Y..

What You Need to Know

Before filing bankruptcy, you can strategically stop paying unsecured debts like credit cards and medical bills. Keep paying your mortgage, car payment, utilities, child support, and alimony. Be aware of the 90-day preference period - large payments to specific creditors before filing can be clawed back. Do not take cash advances or make luxury purchases on credit cards within 70-90 days of filing.

Filed in S.D.N.Y.. Courthouse: One Bowling Green, Manhattan.

Frequently Asked Questions

What Bills Can I Stop Paying Before Filing?

Before filing bankruptcy, you can strategically stop paying unsecured debts like credit cards and medical bills. Keep paying your mortgage, car payment, utilities, child support, and alimony. Be aware of the 90-day preference period - large payments to specific creditors before filing can be clawed back. Do not take cash advances or make luxury purchases on credit cards within 70-90 days of filing.

Does this apply in New York?

Yes. This applies to S.D.N.Y.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

One Bowling Green, Manhattan.
